reg_title: May 2021, are rescinded upon publication of this regulation.

chunks.text:
This regulation prescribes comprehensive alcohol and drug abuse prevention and control policies, proce-
dures, and responsibilities for Soldiers of all components, Department of the Army (DA) Civilians, and
other personnel eligible for Army Substance Abuse Program (ASAP) services. The comprehensive pro-
gram is composed of integrated functions that include deterrence, drug testing, prevention and training,
and referrals. Nothing in this regulation will infringe on the Assistant Secretary of Defense for Health Af-
fairs (ASD(HA)) or the Defense Health Agency’s statutory and regulatory authority. In the event of any
conflict between this regulation and the ASD(HA)’s statutory or regulatory authority, the authoritative law
or policy takes precedence.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-2. References, forms, and explanation of abbreviations
chunks.text:
See appendix A. The abbreviations, brevity codes, and acronyms (ABCAs) used in this electronic publica-
tion are defined when you hover over them. All ABCAs are listed in the ABCA directory located at
https://armypubs.army.mil/.
Procedures associated with this regulation are found in DA Pam 600 - 85.
Responsibilities are listed in chapter 2.

The records management requirement for all record numbers, associated forms, and reports required by
this publication are addressed in the Records Retention Schedule-Army (RRS - A). Detailed information
for all related record numbers, forms, and reports are located in Army Records Information Management
System (ARIMS)/RRS - A at https://www.arims.army.mil. If any record numbers, forms, and reports are not
current, addressed, and/or published correctly in ARIMS/RRS - A, see DA Pam 25 - 403 for guidance.

On 28 September 1971, Public Law (PL) 92 - 129, mandated that the Secretary of Defense develop pro-
grams for the identification (ID), treatment, and rehabilitation of alcohol or other drug-dependent persons
in the Armed Forces. Similarly, PL 91 - 616 and PL 92 - 255 authorized the Secretary of Defense to de-
velop programs for Department of Defense (DoD) Civilians. In turn, the Secretary of Defense requires
each of the Services to develop alcohol and other drug abuse prevention and control programs in accord-
ance with Department of Defense instruction (DoDI) 1010.04, DoDI 1010.01, and DoDI 1010.09. In re-
sponse to these directives, the Army conducts a comprehensive program to prevent and control the
abuse of alcohol and other drugs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text:
Substance abuse contributes to high-risk behaviors, runs counter to Army Values and erodes per-
sonal readiness. ASAP is a mechanism within the DPRR system of support that, when administered ap-
propriately, through engaged and empowered leadership, supports building personal readiness and resili-
ence, and optimizes performance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text:
The command role in substance abuse prevention, drug and alcohol testing, early ID of problems,
and administrative or judicial actions is essential. Commanders will ensure that all officials and supervi-
sors support the ASAP. Proposals to provide ASAP services that deviate from requirements prescribed by
this regulation must be approved by the Deputy Chief of Staff (DCS), G - 9, DPRR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text: The overarching tenets of the ASAP are deterrence, prevention, and treatment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text: The capabilities supporting deterrence are drug testing, ID/detection, and referral.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text:
The capabilities supporting prevention are awareness, education, risk reduction, interventions, and
employee assistance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text:
While not a part of ASAP, SUDCC supports the Army’s strategy to prevent substance abuse within
the Army and provide treatment services when clinically indicated.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text:
Table 1 - 1 depicts this alignment and provides definitions for each capability.
Table 1 - 1
Overarching tenets and supporting capabilities of Army Substance Abuse Program - Continued
Tenets
Capability
Definition
Deterrence
Drug testing
Action or threat of action to be taken in order to dissuade Soldiers or
government employees from abusing or misusing substances. The Army’s
primary mechanism of deterrence is random drug testing.
Prevention
Identification (ID) or detection
The process of identifying Soldiers and other beneficiaries as potential or
actual substance abusers. This ID can be via self-ID, command ID, drug
testing ID, medical ID, investigation, or apprehension ID.
Prevention
Referral
Modes by which Soldiers and other beneficiaries can access garrison ASAP
referral for prevention.
Modes are self-referral and command referral.
Treatment
Referral
An in-depth individual biopsychosocial evaluation interview to assess needs.
Modes are self-referral and command referral. See pertinent TSG and DHA
regulatory guidance.
Prevention
Targeted education
An educational/motivational program that focuses on the adverse effects and
consequences of alcohol and other drug abuse.
Treatment
Counseling Services
Outpatient clinical intervention with the goal of returning Soldiers and/or other
beneficiaries to full duty or identify Soldiers who are not able to successfully
rehabilitate. See pertinent TSG and DHA regulatory guidance.
Prevention
Risk reduction
Compile, analyze, and assess behavioral risk and other data to identify
trends and units with high-risk profiles. Provide systematic prevention and
intervention methods and materials to commanders to eliminate or mitigate
individual high-risk behaviors.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text: The Army maintains the following principles:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text:
Abuse of alcohol, use of illegal drugs and misuse of prescription drugs are inconsistent with Army
values and the standards of performance, discipline, and readiness necessary to accomplish the Army’s
mission.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text:
Unit commanders must intervene early and refer all Soldiers suspected of alcohol or other drug-use
problems to BH for a substance use disorder (SUD) evaluation. The unit commander will support treat-
ment plans for all Soldiers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text:
Participation is mandatory for all Soldiers who are command referred and/or subsequently enrolled
in mandatory treatment. Failure to attend mandatory counseling sessions may constitute a violation of Ar-
ticle 86 of the Uniform Code of Military Justice (UCMJ, Art. 86).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text:
Commanders may initiate administrative separation for Soldiers who fail to participate adequately in
or to respond successfully to treatment. In addition to existing separation policies for alcohol- or other
drug-abuse treatment failures, administrative separation for alcohol or drug abuse failure will be initiated
for Soldiers with a subsequent alcohol or drug-related incident of misconduct at any time during the 12-
month period following treatment or during the 12-month period following removal from the treatment pro-
gram, for any reason. This expanded period does not prevent separation for other reasons authorized by
existing administrative separation regulations or other authorities.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text:
Substance use disorder treatment will be addressed in a single program that is integrated with the
BHSOC to ensure holistic care. Treatment will generally be short term and conducted in a manner that
supports the military environment and the readiness of the force.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text:
An active and aggressive drug and alcohol testing program serves as an effective deterrent against
alcohol and drug abuse.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Army Substance Abuse Program concept and principles
chunks.text:
The military police (MP), U.S. Army Criminal Investigation Command (USACID), and other investi-
gative personnel will not enroll in or otherwise infiltrate substance use disorder treatment for the purpose
of law enforcement activities or to solicit information from Soldiers enrolled in mandatory treatment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Army Substance Abuse Program eligibility criteria
chunks.text:
Services are authorized for personnel who are statutorily eligible to receive ASAP services and/or
who are eligible to receive medical care for substance use disorder treatment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Army Substance Abuse Program eligibility criteria
chunks.text:
When Soldiers are under the administrative jurisdiction of another Service, they will comply with the
alcohol and drug programs of that Service. All drug test results and records of referrals for counseling and
rehabilitation/treatment will be reported through Army alcohol and drug-abuse treatment channels to the
DPRR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Army Substance Abuse Program eligibility criteria
chunks.text:
When elements of the Army and another Service are so located that cost effectiveness, efficiency,
and combat readiness can be achieved by combining facilities, the Service to receive the support will be
responsible for initiating a local Memorandum of Understanding and/or Inter-Service Support Agreement
(refer to DoDI 4000.19).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Army Substance Abuse Program eligibility criteria
chunks.text:
Members of the ARNG and USAR who are on active duty (AD) orders for more than 30 consecutive
days, or those on AD orders with an approved Line of Duty (LOD), are covered for any injury, illness, or
disease incurred or aggravated in the line of duty, are eligible for substance use disorder treatment and
required to follow clinical care policies throughout this regulation.

Activities must meet the applicable labor relations obligations prior to implementing the terms of this regu-
lation as they relate to the conditions of employment of bargaining unit members. Questions regarding
labor relations implications and responsibilities concerning civilian drug testing should be addressed
through the civilian personnel chain of command to the Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1 (DAPE - AR), 300 Army
Pentagon, Washington, DC 20310 - 0300.
Responsibilities

Develop plans, policies, and procedures that implement the provisions of this regulation throughout
the ARNG.
Ensure that ARNG units comply with this regulation.

Advise the DCS, G -9 regarding the impact of alcohol, drug abuse and gambling disorder in the
ARNG.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text:
Plan and manage the execution of civilian and military manpower policies and programs in support of
the ASAP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text:
Ensure that directors of servicing Civilian Human Resources Service Centers support commanders
and supervisors managing DA civilian alcohol testing requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text:
Ensure execution of Department of Transportation (DOT) regulations governing alcohol and sub-
stance abuse testing requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text:
Ensure implementation of EO 12564 for all civilian applicants for employment within the Army and to
maintain the goal of a drug-free workplace.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text:
Ensure that commanders and supervisors comply with EO 12564 for DA civilian employees in testing
designated positions (TDPs).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text:
Advise and assist the Assistant Secretary of the Army (Manpower and Reserve Affairs) to develop
and oversee Army policy that implements the testing of new entrants for substance use or abuse (see
DoDI 1010.01).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text:
Ensure that disciplinary and adverse actions taken against DA civilian employees found to be using
illegal drugs or to be impaired by alcohol while on duty comply with law, federal regulations, and DoDI
1010.09.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text:
Advise and assist the ASA (M&RA) to develop and oversee policy defining the nature and level of
use and abuse of specific substances that violate the conditions of service in the Army, and plan and
manage the execution of personnel actions to address violations, to include actions brought under law,
regulations, and DoD and Army policy. Ensure the execution of Army manpower policies to support the
ASAP including, but not limited to-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text: Suspension of favorable personnel actions (Flags) (see AR 600 - 8 - 2).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text: Administrative separation (see AR 135 - 175, AR 135 - 178, AR 600 - 8 - 24, AR 635 - 200).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text:
Filing, transferring, or removing unfavorable information related to drug or alcohol misuse in a Sol-
dier’s Army Human Resource Record (see AR 600 - 37).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text:
Management of deployed Soldiers with a confirmed positive drug test (see AR 614 - 100, AR
614 - 200).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text:
Limited Use Policy that prohibits the use of protected drug or alcohol-related evidence against a
Soldier in administrative proceedings or actions brought under the Uniform Code of Military Justice.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text: Ensure commanders and supervisors provide Soldiers with sufficient notice of the Limited Use Policy.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 1
chunks.text:
Plan and manage the execution of Army policy governing the transfer of Soldiers receiving substance
use disorder (SUD) treatment to Department of Veteran Affairs treatment facilities.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Plan and manage the execution of all non-clinical policies pertaining to the prevention, resilience,
and readiness elements of the ASAP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text: Direct the Director, Directorate of Prevention, Resilience and Readiness (DPRR) to-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text: Provide guidance and leadership on all non-clinical alcohol and drug policy issues.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text: Exercise staff leadership and supervision over the ASAP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Ensure the Risk Reduction Program (RRP) interfaces with related functional areas within the Direc-
tor of Human Resources Policy’s responsibilities and coordinates RRP activities with other related DoD,
DA, and civilian agencies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text: Oversee the Army’s Drug and Alcohol Testing Program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text: Direct ASAP operations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text: Develop ASAP goals and policies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text: Review, assess, and recommend policy changes, as appropriate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Interpret ASAP policy in response to inquiries from all internal and external government agencies
(including Army commands (ACOMs), Army service component commands (ASCCs), Direct Reporting
Units (DRUs) and their subordinate commands, other uniformed Services, DoD, and other Federal agen-
cies).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Prepare budget submissions, direct allocation of funds, monitor execution of resources, and serve
as the functional budget program manager for the ASAP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Oversee programs, develop plans, formulate budgets, and provide technical assistance and train-
ing for ASAP civilian services.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Maintain liaison between the Army and the other uniformed Services, other Federal agencies, and
the private sector.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Provide operational guidance, monitoring, and oversight of the worldwide ASAP. Coordinate man-
agement, funding, and execution of the ASAP with Army Material Command (AMC), AMC - IMCOM, Na-
tional Guard Bureau (NGB), and the USAR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Consolidate all alcohol and drug statistics and provide periodic reports to the Director, DPRR, the
Army staff, ACOMs, ASCCs, DRUs, DoD, the Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS), and
ASAP managers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Establish and maintain program-level evaluation plans, measures, data collections, analyses, and
reporting requirements for implementation at Army, AMC - IMCOM, ACOM, ASCC, DRU, and installation
levels.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text: Publish an ASAP Evaluation Plan, which will be updated annually, or as ASAP policy changes.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Provide technical assistance in the use of automation and other emerging technologies in sub-
stance abuse programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Develop, establish, administer, and evaluate alcohol, drug abuse and gambling disorder preven-
tion, education, and training programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
DPRR Director will review and approve all ASAP curriculum developed for U. S. Army Training and
Doctrine Command (TRADOC) schools/courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text: Provide training at senior leadership training courses upon request.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Develop, establish, administer, and evaluate alcohol, drug abuse, and gambling disorder training
and educational programs for installation ASAP professional staff. Establish selection criteria and provide
allocations for nominees to attend training sponsored by DA.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text: Maintain staffing inventory data for the ASAP worldwide.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Serve as DA’s lead on all issues related to drug demand reduction (DDR) Programs and alcohol
abuse prevention.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Serve as DA’s proponent for the RRP, which complements the Army Combat Readiness Center
Risk Management process. Direct the operations of the RRP and coordinate RRP policy with appropriate
DoD, DA, and civilian agencies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Serve as the subject matter expert (SME) supporting the Army Civilian Education System with
training development and analysis for all ASAP positions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Ensure DA programs comply with the policies of the Office of National Drug Control Policy and the
National Drug Control Strategy.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text: Provide positive drug test reports to USACID for dissemination across their organization.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Provide guidance regarding alcohol testing, urine collection, chain of custody, handling and ship-
ping, and training of Unit Deterrence Leaders (UDLs) and drug testing coordinators (DTCs).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text: Manage and distribute drug testing quota allocations, as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text:
Approve proposals through the DPRR to provide ASAP non-clinical services that deviate from pro-
cedures prescribed by this regulation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 9
chunks.text: Oversee the duties of the contract officer representative (COR).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-4. Deputy Chief of Staff, G - 3/5/7
chunks.text:
The DCS, G - 3/5/7 will appoint a representative to coordinate RRP policy and statistics with the ASAP
and serve on a Headquarters, Department of the Army (HQDA) risk reduction working group.

Function as the Chief Medical Advisor on matters pertaining to military health readiness require-
ments and safety of Soldiers.

Provide direction and guidance to the Army to recruit, organize, train, and equip military medical per-
sonnel to treat addictive behaviors to include SUDs and gambling disorders.
Develop direction and guidance for SUD medical personnel for SUD treatment.

Provide operational guidance, funding, and management to the Forensic Toxicology Drug Testing
Laboratory (FTDTL) that supports the Army’s Drug and Alcohol Testing Program.

Develop SUDCC metrics; evaluate SUDCC; and coordinate with the Director, DPRR in total program
assessments.
Provide medical review officer (MRO) services for military and civilian personnel drug testing.

Provide Substance Abuse Professional (SAP) services for civilian U.S. Department of Transportation
(DOT) alcohol and drug testing (see DOT guidance at https://www.transportation.gov).

Design and furnish deployment-specific training packages for behavioral health and combat stress
control medical units.
Evaluate the legal aspects of the ASAP.

Review laboratory forensic specimen handling procedures (chain of custody) and other drug and al-
cohol testing program elements for legal sufficiency when requested.
Appoint a liaison to the DPRR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct reporting
chunks.text:
Appoint a staff officer to serve as liaison with DPRR on substance abuse issues and may request
access to substance abuse compliance metric databases that support report reviews.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct reporting
chunks.text:
Appoint a representative to coordinate the RRP, its policies and statistics with the DPRR and serve
on a HQDA risk reduction working group.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct reporting
chunks.text: During prolonged deployments-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct reporting
chunks.text: Determine optimal number of base area codes (BACs) and their alignment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct reporting
chunks.text: Provide detailed policy concerning random testing expectations and limitations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Commanders of Army commands, Army service component commands, and direct reporting
chunks.text:
Ensure ASAP capabilities are addressed in the personnel and/or medical operations plan or annex
for deployments. Minimum services would include drug testing and clinical assessment; however, based
on mission, enemy, terrain, troops, time, civil considerations (METT - TC) and security, additional services
may need to be provided.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Commanding General, U. S. Army Training and Doctrine Command
chunks.text:
The CG, TRADOC will ensure that current and appropriate training on abuse of substances (illegal drug,
controlled drug, alcohol or other), gambling disorder awareness, and information on the ASAP occurs at
initial entry, pre-commissioning and is integrated into all other Army professional development courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text:
Provide guidance and leadership on the execution of the deterrence and prevention functions which
includes substance abuse prevention and suicide prevention.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text:
Resource and staff the installation ASAP and support installation programs to achieve program ob-
jectives and to respond to the needs of commanders and supervisors.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text: Coordinate and monitor the implementation of installation drug and alcohol testing programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text: Appoint a staff officer to serve as a liaison with the DPRR on substance abuse issues.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text:
Establish and implement supporting and supplemental plans consistent with the objectives and pro-
cedures established by the ASAP evaluation plan.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text:
Prepare AMC - IMCOM ASAP program objective memorandum and budget submissions, monitor exe-
cution of management decision evaluation packages (MDEPs), MDEP code for the ASAP funds and the
Quality Assurance Assessment Program (QAAP) and MDEP code for DoD Counternarcotic funds (VCND)
allocated to AMC - IMCOM, and coordinate ASAP resource management with the AMC - IMCOM Chief,
ASAP/R2.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text:
Monitor the installation employee assistance program (EAP) and keep the Director, DPRR updated
regarding all ASAP civilian services and related statistical data.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text:
Conduct and provide DPRR, via AMC the results of a 25% random sample review of AD Soldiers an-
nually to determine if required substance misuse prevention training is received annually. Provide results
of the annual training review to ARD no later than 1 April of each year.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text: Collect and maintain necessary management information to assess program effectiveness.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text: Appoint a representative who will serve on the HQDA risk reduction working group.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text:
Ensure all installations with over 500 Regular Army Soldiers appoint a representative to coordinate
the RRP policies and statistics with the DPRR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text:
Serve as an information resource to ACOMs, ASCCs, and DRUs on substance abuse issues for their
units.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text:
Serve as liaison between ASAP managers and the Director, DPRR on matters pertaining to ASAP
manpower, budget, and administration.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text:
Ensure that installation programs are executing their responsibilities to provide substance abuse and
gambling disorder prevention, education, and training to sustain and improve the skills and abilities of the
ASAP installation staff in accordance with chapter 9.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Commanding General, U.S. Army Material Command-Army Installation Management
chunks.text:
Allocate and monitor utilization of all available urinalysis (UA) quotas within the AMC - IMCOM, as
required.

Develop and execute plans, policies, and procedures of the USAR ASAP in coordination with the Di-
rector, DPRR.

Recommend policies and operational tasks to the DCS, G - 1 regarding the participation of USAR
Soldiers and their Families in the ASAP. (See chap 16 for specific USAR guidance.)
Ensure USAR units comply with this regulation.

Conduct, compile, and provide DPRR the results of a 25% random sample review of USAR Soldiers
annually to determine if required substance misuse prevention training was received during the previous
calendar year. Provide results of the annual training review to ARD no later than 1 April of each year.

Advise the DCS, G - 9 regarding the impact of alcohol, drug misuse and problematic gambling in the
USAR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-11. Director, U.S. Army Criminal Investigation Command
chunks.text:
Conduct and support operations, programs, and activities designed to deter, prevent, and suppress
traffic in controlled substances in accordance with AR 190 - 30.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-11. Director, U.S. Army Criminal Investigation Command
chunks.text:
Provide periodic drug assessment reports to the DCS, G - 9 (who has responsibilities for ASAP pro-
gram management) in accordance with AR 190 - 45.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-12. Commander, U.S. Army Corps of Engineers
chunks.text:
The Commander, USACE will promulgate a USACE regulation that prescribes USACE-specific policies
and procedures, and assigns responsibilities to implement the ASAP within the USACE. The provisions of
the USACE regulation will comply with the policies and programs prescribed in this regulation. The Com-
mander, USACE will submit the draft USACE regulation and any future revisions of the regulation to the
ASA (M&RA) for approval.

The DASAF will appoint a representative to coordinate RRP policy and statistics with the DPRR and
serve on a HQDA risk reduction working group.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Commanders of medical readiness commands
chunks.text:
Provide oversight for the SUDCC activities staffed by Medical Treatment Facilities within the MRC’s
’s area of responsibility.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Commanders of medical readiness commands
chunks.text:
Ensure medical resources are available to conduct the required medical review of military and civil-
ian drug tests results to include deployed areas.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-14. Commanders of medical readiness commands
chunks.text:
Ensure a sufficient number of providers are eligible to serve as MROs who are both trained and certi-
fied prior to deployment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text:
Ensure subordinate commanders execute the military Drug Testing Program (DTP), in accordance
with chapter 4, during the course of their command or Organizational Inspection Programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text:
Ensure battalion commanders appoint officers or noncommissioned officers (NCOs) (E - 5 promota-
ble or above) on orders as battalion deterrence leaders (BDLs) and alternate UDLs to perform the duties
listed in paragraph 2 - 32.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text:
Ensure units are prepared to conduct drug testing while on temporary duty (TDY), deployed, or areas
of operation as required in paragraph 4 - 7.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text:
Ensure participation in and directing subordinate commanders to participate in RRP command con-
sultations provided by the installation risk reduction program coordinator (RRPC) or Installation Preven-
tion Plan (IPP) members.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text:
Designate a representative to present RRP-related issues or requests to the installation or senior
commander.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text:
Ensure the unit risk inventory (URI) is administered to all Soldiers at least 120 days before an opera-
tional deployment and the re-integration unit risk inventory (R - URI) is administered to all Soldiers be-
tween 30 and 180 days after returning from an operational deployment (see para 12 - 6).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text:
Recommend subordinate commanders use the URI during changes of command to identify high risk
behaviors within their units.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text:
Ensure subordinate commanders fulfill the unit prevention and education requirements required in
paragraph 2 - 29.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text:
Ensure Training Officers work with the ASAP to review a random sampling of Soldiers to determine if
annual substance misuse prevention training was received during the previous calendar year.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text: Deglamorize the use of all forms of illegal drugs, prescription drugs, and irresponsible use of alcohol.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text:
Ensure subordinate commanders refer Soldiers to BH for a SUD evaluation within 5 working days of
notification that the Soldiers received positive UA results for illicit drug use or were involved in alcohol-
related misconduct. Ensure unit commanders support Soldier’s treatment plan including level of care
needed to return Soldier to full duty status.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text: During extended deployments and areas of operations-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text: Assign and certify personnel to serve as primary and alternate base area code managers (BACM).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text:
Provide guidance to subordinate commanders concerning random drug testing expectations and
temporary modifications to testing rates, as applicable.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-15. Commanders of corps, divisions, and brigades
chunks.text:
Ensure the first general officer (GO) in the chain of command with a judge advocate or legal advisor
receives the appropriate information on Soldiers with positive drug test results and makes the retention
decision when required in accordance with paragraph 10 - 6 before it goes to the General Court-Martial
Convening Authority.

MTF commanders will execute roles and responsibilities in compliance with DoDD 5136.13 and other ap-
plicable policies.

Establish a local command ASAP and ensure the full range of ASAP services are available to all eli-
gible personnel. The installation elements of the ASAP should be operationally integrated to achieve max-
imum command/Soldier readiness.
Designate each of the following positions:

An ASAP manager to function as the installation ASAP single point of contact (POC) for administra-
tive functions of the installation ASAP. The ASAP manager will also work with the Installation Director of
Psychological Health and/or designed SUD staff to provide effective and efficient integration of the instal-
lation ASAP tenets and clinical care.
A prevention coordinator (PC) to administer the prevention and education functions.

An employee assistance program coordinator (EAPC) to administer the ASAP civilian assistance
services.
A DTC to administer the drug and alcohol testing program.

An installation breath alcohol technician (BAT) (in the continental United States, Hawaii, Alaska, and
Puerto Rico) to instruct and assist individuals in the alcohol testing process and to operate a breath test-
ing device in accordance with DOT guidelines.
A RRPC, when required by paragraph 12-3a, to facilitate risk reduction activities.

Establish an installation prevention team (IPT), human resources council, or a similar appropriate fo-
rum to focus on installation substance abuse misuse and risk reduction issues. Use this forum to develop
and implement an approved IPP to address the issues identified. Serve as chairperson of the IPT or hu-
man resources council and ensure the following are represented: chaplain, preventive medicine, medical
department activity (MEDDAC), behavioral health (which may include SUDCC staff), installation safety
office, RRPC, provost marshal (PM), ASAP manager, PC, CID, legal, and suicide prevention. The senior
commander has the authority to adjust the membership, as required. Any council, team, or committee es-
tablished will develop and implement a formal charter in accordance with AR 15 - 1.

Exercise direct supervision of the installation ASAP manager through the Director, Human Re-
sources.
Appoint an installation designated management official (DMO) on orders to manage the civilian DTP.
Notify the Installation Director of Psychological Health (IDPH) of any indications that treatment func-

Develop a mutual support plan among the installation ASAP, PM, and Criminal Investigation Division
(CID) to include-

Immediately report all offenses involving illegal possession, use, sale, or trafficking in drugs or drug
paraphernalia to the Criminal Investigation Division (CID) for investigation.

The PM provides the ASAP manager with extracts from Army Law Enforcement Reporting and
Tracking System (ALERTS) on all incidents involving abuse of alcohol, drugs, or other substance on a
daily basis.
Support positive and non-attributional approaches to risk reduction.
Facilitate business processes and structures to support the RRP, as required.
Evaluate IPPs annually.
Maintain the means to perform alcohol breath tests on Soldiers and DA Civilians.
Publish a command policy memorandum that addresses alcohol and drug abuse.

In joint basing where common services are provided by one branch of the military, complete a mem-
orandum of agreement with counterpart that specifies which Service will provide each of the necessary
ASAP services.

Continuous command presence in installation living, working, and recreational areas to reduce alco-
hol and drug misuse.

Deglamorize the use of all forms of illegal drugs, prescription drug misuse and irresponsible use of
alcohol.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Provide direct supervision and management over all installation drug testing and prevention/educa-
tion components of ASAP staff and programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text: Prepare installation ASAP budget submissions and monitor execution of the funding.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Develop, coordinate, and recommend local installation ASAP policies and procedures for implemen-
tation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Manage and monitor the drug and alcohol testing program (see chaps 3, 4, and 5 of this regulation
for information on specific requirements related to the military and civilian alcohol and drug testing). En-
sure the Drug Testing Collection Point (DTCP) standard operating procedures (SOPs) are reviewed annu-
ally.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Serve as the coordinator of all substance abuse and risk reduction issues for the IPT, human re-
sources council, or other similar appropriate forums.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Monitor and report as recorded in Drug and Alcohol Management Information System (DAMIS) the
commander referral rate, the screening rate, and provide quarterly reports to the installation, battalion
commanders and the AMC - IMCOM Chief, ASAP/R2.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Ensure there is a continuous and comprehensive ASAP staff training plan for all installation staff to
enhance professional skills.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Establish communications, a referral network, and administrative coordination between military units
and civilian activities and the MTF BH department to facilitate the effectiveness of SUDs treatment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Assist commanders and supervisors in the ID and referral of individuals suspected of problematic
substance use to include tracking alcohol and drug abuse prevention training (ADAPT) attendance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Maintain installation ASAP and EAP records and authenticate all installation ASAP reports furnished
to higher HQ.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Institute procedures and strategies designed to enhance the deterrent effect of drug and alcohol test-
ing.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Consult with the IDPH, local law enforcement personnel, and other installation personnel in designing
and implementing the IPP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Using input from the PC, evaluate all prevention education and training aspects of the local ASAP at
the end of the fiscal year, and forward through the Commander, AMC - IMCOM, and AMC to the Director,
DPRR, a written report of the installation prevention program activities and accomplishments.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
For drug test results identified as ‘At Risk’ Servicemember, notify the MRO to expedite the review
within 24 hours of notification. Send an email regarding the initiation of all FENT/NFENT related ‘At Risk’
expedited reviews to the Army Forensic Toxicology Program Manager and Deputy Program Manager.
Once reviews are completed, notify the unit commander and advise immediate referral of the Soldier to
SUDCC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text: For military personnel only, restrict notification of positive drug test results to-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text: The commander who ordered the test.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text: The chain of command over the commander who ordered the test.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text: The supporting legal office when they are acting on behalf of the commander who ordered the test.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
The IDPH and/or clinical director (CD) on all positive drug testing; for rehabilitation tests results, the
CD or assigned SUD provider will report results.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Ensure ASAP facilitate background checks as appropriate to support the Army’s criteria for determin-
ing suitability for selection and assignment of Soldiers and civilians for schools and to positions of trust.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Provide policy guidance and assistance to the servicing Civilian Human Resources Service Center
(CHRSC) (CPAC) to identify all drug-free federal workplace (DFW) testing designated positions (TDP) s
and those positions subject to DOT drug and alcohol testing rules at least quarterly and with all supervi-
sors at least annually.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Serve as the primary DMO for verified positive drug test results for DA civilian employees in accord-
ance with 49 CFR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Adhere to guidance for the TDPs as provided in chapter 5. Refer to DA Pamphlet (Pam) 600 - 85 for
additional instructions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text: Maintain ASAP statistics as directed by Director, DPRR (see chaps 4, 5, 6 and chap 14).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Work with Unit Training Officers to review a random sampling of assigned Soldiers each year to de-
termine if substance misuse prevention training was received during the previous calendar year. Results
will be reported to AMC - IMCOM.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Collect and maintain data on the status of civilian employees’ and Family members’ participation in
the garrison or non-clinical ASAP and provide reports, as required.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Promptly furnish extracts from the daily MP desk blotter to the IDPH on all incidents involving alco-
hol, drugs, and other substance abuse.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Appoint a primary and alternate DTC on orders and ensure they are trained and certified through the
DA DTC certification course.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Assess the installation ASAP on an annual basis using the guide at appendix D. Inspect at least one
of the four DTC functional areas on a quarterly basis. Record all assessments and inspection findings on
a memorandum for record (MFR) and maintain in accordance with AR 25 - 400 - 2. Assess the installation
ASAP in accordance with AR 11 - 2 every 5 years using the guide at appendix G.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-18. Installation Army Substance Abuse Program managers
chunks.text:
Supervise the MRO review process and ensure the review timelines in chapter 4 are met.
aa. Prepare and submit all required reports in DAMIS or other electronic form as specified in chapter
14.
bb. If the installation has personnel who require drug testing under DOT rules, ensure the ASAP has
the capability to perform these UA collections in accordance with DOT guidelines.
cc. Ensure that a Soldier’s DAMIS record is reviewed after receiving a positive result for illegal sub-
stances and that the Soldier's company commander, as well as the first GO in the chain of command, is
notified of all positive UA results in the Soldier's record.
dd. Report all drug positives within 5 working days of FTDL notification to the Soldier’s Commander.
ee. Notify unit commanders of the requirement for Soldiers with positive drug test results, substance
misuse related incidents, and alcohol related incidents reported on the Army Law Enforcement Reporting
Tracking System to be referred to ADAPT within 5 working days of the incident. Referred Soldiers must
complete ADAPT within 60 days of reported incident.
ff. Monitor all ADAPT referrals and document completion in the DAMIS.
Promote ASAP and SUDCC services using marketing, networking, and consulting strategies.

Provide training and any other services to assist organizations in ensuring all military and civilian per-
sonnel are provided prevention education training. The DOT-designated positions and other high risk ci-
vilian positions should receive targeted training pertaining to their jobs. The PCs will track all training con-
ducted by unit or directorate, as appropriate.

Support units’ efforts to conduct random sample review of Soldiers to determine if required sub-
stance misuse prevention training is received annually. Provide results of random sample to
AMC - IMCOM to be compiled and reported to DPRR.

Coordinate with the installation training officer to assist in integrating the preventive education and
training efforts into the overall installation training program.

Design, develop, and administer target group-oriented alcohol and other drug abuse prevention edu-
cation and training programs in coordination with the ASAP staff and other installation prevention profes-
sionals.

Maintain liaison with schools serving military Family members, civic organizations, civilian agencies,
and military organizations to integrate the efforts of all community preventive education resources.
Oversee the UDL training program. Provide UDLs with education and training materials.

Maintain lists of available continuing education and training courses and workshops provided by
ASAP, AMC - IMCOM, and appropriate civilian agencies for ASAP installation staff and coordinate alloca-
tions for military and civilian training courses through the AMC - IMCOM.
Address military community risk levels and work toward reducing the risk factors.
Maintain class rosters for all training and track substance abuse training on the installation by unit.
Conduct pre- and post-deployment, TDY, and area of operation substance abuse training.

Teach the ADAPT course to all individuals with a positive UA and indicated prevention requirement
(see chap 9 for specific requirements).
To the extent possible, teach at least one substance misuse prevention class to each unit per year.
Develop, in consultation with ASAP staff members, a substance abuse prevention plan annually.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Installation employee assistance program coordinators
chunks.text:
Assess, plan, and establish local procedures for providing comprehensive EAP services for eligible
DA civilian employees and military and civilian Family members within the military community (refer to DA
Pam 600 - 85 for a discussion of comprehensive EAP services).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Installation employee assistance program coordinators
chunks.text:
Provide screening, assessment, short-term counseling, and referral services for employees who self-
refer or whom management refers. Providing short-term guidance, education, and mediation to civilian
employees for resolution of work-related and non-work-related productivity problems associated with em-
ployees impaired by personal concerns including, but not limited to, health, marital, family, financial, alco-
hol, drug, legal, emotional, and other personal concerns which may adversely affect employee job perfor-
mance. If clinical counseling is indicated, the EAPC will make a referral to a BH clinic or to a referral
source in the local civilian community.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Installation employee assistance program coordinators
chunks.text:
Conduct evaluation of EAP services to ensure effectiveness of referrals and resolution of personal
concerns, as needed.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Installation employee assistance program coordinators
chunks.text:
Advise and update supervisors concerning their employees’ progress to the extent permitted by par-
agraph 6 - 8 and any applicable law.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Installation employee assistance program coordinators
chunks.text:
Consult with the installation Civilian Human Resources Service Center (CHRSC) , SAP, and supervi-
sors of DA Civilian employees throughout the installation within the limits required by Title 42 United
States Code (USC) 290dd-2 and 42 CFR Part 2.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Installation employee assistance program coordinators
chunks.text:
Maintain an updated list of available community counseling and rehabilitation/treatment resources
that address the full spectrum of work-related and non-work-related productivity problems associated with
employees impaired by personal concerns.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Installation employee assistance program coordinators
chunks.text:
Coordinate with the PC on prevention education and training for supervisors and DA Civilian employ-
ees on abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled drug, alcohol or other) and gambling disorder aware-
ness and appropriate information on work-related and non-work-related productivity problems associated
with employees impaired by personal concerns that are specific to the needs of the population serviced
(refer to DA Pam 600 - 85 for employee education and supervisory training prerequisites). Civilian person-
nel may receive prevention education as mission needs dictate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Installation employee assistance program coordinators
chunks.text: Publicize and market ASAP services available for civilian employees.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Installation employee assistance program coordinators
chunks.text:
Assist the PC in developing and executing prevention campaigns and conducting education and pre-
vention programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Installation employee assistance program coordinators
chunks.text: Collect information required for reports.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-20. Installation employee assistance program coordinators
chunks.text:
Maintain EAP files in accordance with the EAP Guidebook and all Federal laws governing the confi-
dentiality of records.
Operate an installation drug and alcohol testing program control point.
Serve as the installation SME on urinalysis (UA) collection and testing.
Augment the installation Inspector General (IG) inspection teams.

Ensure that urine collections from Soldiers are performed, as required, in accordance with chapter 4
and appendix D.

Teach the drug testing procedures portion of the UDL certification course and, in coordination with
the PC, provide pre- and post-deployment training to UDLs.
Advise unit commanders and the ASAP managers on test procedures and results.
Manage drug testing supplies and expenditures.

Ensure the substance abuse programs and UA collection procedures of all units are inspected in ac-
cordance with chapter 4.
Be prepared to testify as an expert witness about the UA collection process during courts-martial.

Maintain drug testing records in accordance with AR 25 - 400 - 2 while keeping military and civilian
documentation in separate filing cabinets.

Retrieve Soldiers’ drug test results from the FTDTL Web portal, and notify the commanders who or-
dered the tests within 5 workdays. The commander must be notified within 24 hours if the test results
identify the Soldier as ‘At-Risk.’

For drug test results identified as ‘At Risk’ Servicemember, notify the MRO to expedite the review
within 24 hours of notification. Send an email regarding the initiation of all FENT/NFENT related ‘At Risk’
expedited reviews to the Army Forensic Toxicology Program Manager and Deputy Program Manager.
Once reviews are completed, notify the unit commander and advise immediate referral of the Soldier to
SUDCC. For any positive results, review the Soldiers’ past UA records in DAMIS to determine if they have
previous positive UA results.

Notify the Soldiers’ company commanders of all positive UA results in the Soldier's DAMIS record.
In addition, the DTC will provide the commander a list of the required actions to take on the Soldier (refer-
ral to BH, referral to ADAPT, suspension of favorable actions (Flag), process for separation, and so forth).

Ensure law enforcement only receives the BAC, UIC, laboratory accession number, specimen col-
lection date, specimen laboratory report date, test basis, and the illicitly used drug(s). Individual Soldier
drug testing and treatment histories will not be provided.
Initiate medical review process for drug positive results requiring such in accordance with chapter 4.

Maintain the installation/command drug testing SOP and ensure that the ASAP manager reviews it
annually and the legal office supporting installation reviews it when changes are made to the SOP docu-
ment.
Conduct drug testing background check on UDL candidates.

The IDPH and/or CD on all positive drug testing; for rehabilitation tests results, the CD or assigned
SUD provider will report results.
Manage installation drug testing quotas.
Manage UDL inquiries to DA and DoD.

Ensure a 3% or less discrepancy rate (including laboratory fatal and non-fatal, and installation speci-
men rejections) is maintained.
Utilize Drug Testing Technician to assists with drug testing duties.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Installation Risk Reduction Program coordinators
chunks.text: Coordinate and facilitate RRP data collection and analysis and Web-based applications, as needed.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Installation Risk Reduction Program coordinators
chunks.text:
Review RRP data and analysis with commanders and coordinate appropriate prevention/intervention
services.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Installation Risk Reduction Program coordinators
chunks.text: Develop, coordinate, and recommend local RRP policies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Installation Risk Reduction Program coordinators
chunks.text: Serve as the coordinator of all RRP issues for the IPT, human resources council, or similar forum.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Installation Risk Reduction Program coordinators
chunks.text:
Ensure RRP data is collected from the data providers monthly and input the data into the ASAP
Web-based system by the 10thth of the month following the previous month. The RRPC has the overall
responsibility in terms of ensuring the data's accuracy.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Installation Risk Reduction Program coordinators
chunks.text:
Assist commanders with identifying high-risk units, conducting URI and R - URI surveys, and identify-
ing appropriate intervention service. Ensure that the URI is administered to all Soldiers at least 120 days
before an operational deployment, and the R - URI is administered to all Soldiers between 30 and 180
days after returning from an operational deployment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Installation Risk Reduction Program coordinators
chunks.text: Institute procedures and strategies designed to enhance RRP visibility on the installation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Installation Risk Reduction Program coordinators
chunks.text: Ensure that RRP responsibilities are being met in support of unit deployment cycles.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Installation Risk Reduction Program coordinators
chunks.text:
Control access to the RRP Web portal by installation personnel, and keep all installation-level POC
information on the Web portal updated.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Installation Risk Reduction Program coordinators
chunks.text:
Coordinate and ensure the IPT meets at least quarterly to discuss prevention issues affecting the en-
tire installation community.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Installation Risk Reduction Program coordinators
chunks.text:
Assist the PC in the development and implementation of an IPP, in collaboration with the IPT. The
IPT may be combined with other installation councils or prevention teams as directed by the senior com-
mander as long as risk reduction is incorporated, and issues are discussed.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-22. Installation Risk Reduction Program coordinators
chunks.text:
Actively participate in the development of prevention programs, supporting other agencies when
called upon.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Installation Director of Psychological Health
chunks.text:
Monitor SUD related trends within the installation, such as acute psychiatric admissions, serious inci-
dent reports (SIRs), or utilization of SUD services. Common techniques used to monitor SUD related
trends include frequent discussion with installation HQs and unit leaders, garrison councils (such as the
Commander’s Ready and Resilient Council (CR2C) and the Risk Reduction Committee).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Installation Director of Psychological Health
chunks.text:
Provide peer review for SUD providers to ensure adherence to clinical substance abuse documenta-
tion standards and report findings to SUD counselors and/or supervisor, as needed.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Installation Director of Psychological Health
chunks.text:
Provide regular feedback on the major SUD related trends to the installation HQs and unit command-
ers and key staff.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Installation Director of Psychological Health
chunks.text: Maintain visibility on the medical readiness of installation Servicemembers with SUD diagnoses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Installation Director of Psychological Health
chunks.text:
Request DCS, G - 1 grant Behavioral Health Division personnel full access to the DAMIS system so
that they can direct SUDCC directors to complete a secondary review of mandatory referrals and enroll-
ment information monthly.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Installation Director of Psychological Health
chunks.text:
Ensure data elements are entered in DAMIS in a timely manner. This includes documenting ADAPT
referrals in DAMIS within three days of Soldiers’ enrollment in SUDCC or disposition of evaluation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Installation Director of Psychological Health
chunks.text:
Establish and maintain rapport with installation HQs leaders, ASAP prevention, education, and drug
testing personnel.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Installation Director of Psychological Health
chunks.text:
Appoint a SUD provider to serve as a member of the Family Advocacy Case Review Committee and
the installation Fatality Review Board to serve as liaison and provide information as appropriate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Installation Director of Psychological Health
chunks.text:
Assess the MTF SUD execution on an annual basis using the guide at appendix E. Record all as-
sessments and inspection findings on a MFR and maintain in accordance with AR 25 - 400 - 2.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Installation Director of Psychological Health
chunks.text:
In accordance with local MTF and BH department policy, participate in at-risk/high interest collabora-
tive care meetings to review individual safety, unit trends, and impacts on mission readiness.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Installation Director of Psychological Health
chunks.text:
Ensure SUD providers attend all required team meetings, including the daily morning meeting and
the weekly Multidisciplinary Psychological Health (MDTP) meetings.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Installation Director of Psychological Health
chunks.text:
Medical supervisors will ensure that MTF privileging committees are notified for determination of ap-
propriate actions to take to maintain medical standards of practice in SUD care.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-23. Installation Director of Psychological Health
chunks.text:
Monitor MEDCOM SUD metrics and report any issues and concerns to MTF Commander for action.
Submit reports through the MTF Commander, through MRC, to OTSG/MEDCOM.

Screen all incident reports for all incidents involving alcohol, drug, or other substance abuse and pro-
vide the ASAP manager with extracts from ALERTS in accordance with AR 190 - 45 on a daily basis.
Support the ASAP manager on matters pertaining to the alcohol testing of DOT designated positions.
Provide quarterly RRP data to the installation ASAP manager or RRPC.

Coordinate with the ASAP manager and provide data on the incidence of alcohol and/or drug in-
volvement in accidents or other safety mishaps.

Inspect installation DTCPs annually for the presence of necessary safety equipment and compliance
with applicable safety regulations and local requirements.
Provide quarterly RRP data to the installation ASAP manager or RRPC.

The installation physical security officers will inspect installation drug and alcohol test collection points bi-
ennially to ensure they meet the requirements for storing UA specimens and records in accordance with
this regulation.
Support the data collection and analysis efforts of the RRP.

Review prevention/intervention methods and materials in their areas of expertise with commanders
to prevent and resolve Soldiers’ high-risk behaviors.
Meet quarterly to discuss the RRP and address prevention issues that affect the installation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Civilian Human Resourcing Servicing Center
chunks.text:
Code management-identified Drug Free Workplace (DFW) TDPs, and those positions subject to
DOT drug and alcohol testing rules in the Defense Civilian Personnel Data System (DCPDS) or other suc-
cessor data system.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Civilian Human Resourcing Servicing Center
chunks.text:
Once concurrence has been obtained from the serviced organization, ensure position descriptions
and vacancy announcements contain appropriate language about random alcohol (for DOT covered posi-
tions) and drug testing conditions of employment for Drug-Free Workplace TDPs identified by the appro-
priate command or management officials.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Civilian Human Resourcing Servicing Center
chunks.text:
Ensure that the completed DA Form 5019 (Condition of Employment for Certain Civilian Positions
Identified Critical under the Department of the Army Drug-Free Federal Workplace Program) and DA
Form 7412 (Condition of Employment for Certain Civilian Positions Identified Safety-Sensitive Under the
Department of Transportation, Federal Highway Administration Rules on Drug and Alcohol Testing) are
filed in the employee's electronic Official Personnel Folder.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Civilian Human Resourcing Servicing Center
chunks.text:
Provide assistance to management when an employee has a confirmed positive drug test under the
DFW testing program and/or has engaged in DOT-prohibited conduct described in 49 CFR Part 382.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Civilian Human Resourcing Servicing Center
chunks.text: Ensure that employees assigned to TDPs complete the following as required:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Civilian Human Resourcing Servicing Center
chunks.text: DA Form 5019.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Civilian Human Resourcing Servicing Center
chunks.text: DA Form 7412.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Civilian Human Resourcing Servicing Center
chunks.text: Ensure the employee, supervisor, and ASAP manager receive copies of the completed forms.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Civilian Human Resourcing Servicing Center
chunks.text:
Provide a roster, which identifies all personnel who occupy DFW TDPs and personnel who are sub-
ject to DOT-regulated drug and alcohol testing, to the installation ASAP manager and U.S. Army Reserve
Command (USARC) DCS, G - 9, if applicable, at least once each quarter with any updates. The roster will
contain at a minimum, the employee’ name, position, title, department/directorate assigned, and supervi-
sor or POC for testing notification purposes.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-28. Civilian Human Resourcing Servicing Center
chunks.text: Refer to DA Pam 600 - 85 for additional instructions for the CHRSC.
Implement a battalion/squadron drug and alcohol testing program (see chap 4 for guidance).
Implement ASAP prevention and education initiatives (see chap 9 of this publication for guidance).

Appoint an officer or NCO (E - 5 promotable or above) on orders as the BDL and alternate BDL, who
must be certified through the UDL training (see 6).

Ensure all newly assigned Soldiers are briefed on ASAP policies and services within 30 days of arri-
val.
Maintain liaison with installation ASAP and BH including SUD staffs.

Maintain ASAP elements while TDY, deployed, and area of operation to the maximum extent possi-
ble. (See para 4 - 7 of this publication for details.) Ensure subordinate units are prepared to conduct drug
testing while TDY, deployed, and area of operation in accordance with paragraph 4 - 7.

Foster a positive command climate that discourages abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled
drug, alcohol or other) and problematic gambling and is supportive of those who need assistance from
behavioral health (BH/SUDCC). Support substance abuse prevention campaigns and alcohol-free activi-
ties in the unit and on the installation.

Initiate administrative separation in accordance with AR 635 - 200, AR 600 - 8 - 24, and paragraph
10 - 6.

Ensure that the URI is administered to all Soldiers at least 120 days before an operational deploy-
ment and the R - URI is administered to all Soldiers between 30 and 180 days after returning from an op-
erational deployment (see para 12 - 6 of this publication).

Immediately report all offenses involving illegal possession, sale, or trafficking in drugs or drug para-
phernalia to the CID. Commanders are no longer required to report positive UA results to local law en-
forcement. This does not alleviate them of the requirement to initiate administrative separation, refer the
Soldier to ADAPT, and BH for a SUD evaluation by completing DA Form 8003 (Command Referral for a
Substance Use Disorder (SUD) Evaluation).

Ensure company commanders refer any Soldier to BH for a SUD evaluation within 5 duty days of no-
tification that the Soldier received a positive UA for illicit drug use or was involved in a problematic alcohol
incident. Commanders of geographically-remote units should contact the IDPH of the nearest installation
for guidance. Ensure company commanders refer any Soldier to BH-Family Advocacy Program (FAP) im-
mediately when drug abuse is related to a child abuse or domestic abuse altercation.

Ensure commanders refer Soldiers who were involved in alcohol-related incidents or had positive
drug test results to ADAPT. Soldier must complete ADAPT within 60 calendar days of reported incident.

Assist the BDL in the development of a battalion/squadron substance abuse program SOP and re-
view and sign it annually. Direct BDL to conduct inspection of subordinate units annually and provide re-
sults to the DTC.

Consider participating in RRP command consultations provided by the installation RRPC or IPT
members.

Bring or designate a representative to bring RRP-related issues or requests to the attention of the
installation or senior commander and RRPC.

Ensure that the URI is administered to all Soldiers at least 120 days before an operational deploy-
ment and the R - URI is administered to all Soldiers between 30 and 180 days after returning from an op-
erational deployment (see para 12 - 6 of this publication).

Recommend subordinate commanders use the URI during changes of command to identify high risk
behaviors within their units.

Battalion commanders will ensure that the initiation and final disposition for all Soldiers with an illicit
positive drug test and Soldiers involved in two serious incidents of alcohol-related misconduct within 12
months is reported to the ASAP manager. In addition, battalion commanders will ensure that a DA Form
4833 (Commander’s Report of Disciplinary or Administrative Action) is completed and submitted in ac-
cordance with AR 190 - 45.
Consult with the servicing legal office for all drug and alcohol related offenses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
Assist the battalion commander in implementing the battalion drug and alcohol testing program (see
chap 4 for guidance).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
All Soldiers, Commanders, and Leaders (Squad Leaders and above) will receive face-to-face sub-
stance misuse prevention training annually. Commanders will determine the duration, location, and
means for conducting training and implement risk reduction measures based on unit assessments. (see
chap 9 of this publication for guidance).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text: Implement ASAP prevention and education initiatives addressed in chapter 9.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
Appoint an officer or NCO (E - 5 or above) on orders as the UDL and alternate UDL, who must be
certified through the UDL training addressed in chapter 9 of this publication.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
Maintain documentation indicating all newly assigned Soldiers have been briefed on ASAP policies
and services within 30 days of arrival.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text: Maintain liaison with ASAP and SUD staffs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
Maintain ASAP elements while deployed, to the maximum extent possible (see para 4 - 7 of this pub-
lication for details).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
Foster a positive command climate that discourages abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled
drug, alcohol or other) and gambling disorder and is supportive of those who need assistance. Support
substance abuse prevention campaigns and alcohol-free activities in the unit and on the installation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text: Consult with the servicing legal office for all drug and alcohol related offenses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
Immediately report all offenses involving illegal possession, sale, or trafficking in drugs or drug para-
phernalia to CID. Commanders are no longer required to report positive UA results to local law enforce-
ment; however, this does not alleviate commanders of the requirement to: Flag the Soldier in accordance
with paragraph 10 - 6, refer the Soldier to ADAPT, and to BH for a SUD evaluation and possible treatment
by completing DA Form 8003.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
Commanders will report the initiation and final disposition for all Soldiers with an illicit positive drug
test and Soldiers involved in two serious incidents of alcohol-related misconduct within 12 months to the
ASAP manager. In addition, commanders must complete and submit DA Form 4833 in accordance with
AR 190 - 45.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
Ensure that Soldiers promptly provide medical evidence for legitimate use of a prescribed drug to the
MRO when requested.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
Refer any Soldier to BH for SUD evaluation within 5 duty days of notification that the Soldier re-
ceived a positive UA for illicit drug use or was involved in alcohol-related misconduct. Commanders of ge-
ographically-remote units should contact IDPH of the nearest installation for guidance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
Refer Soldiers who were involved in alcohol-related incidents or had positive drug test results to
ADAPT. Soldiers must complete ADAPT within 60 calendar days of reported incident.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
Assist the UDL in the development of a unit problematic substance use program SOP and sign it at
least annually.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
Initiate separation for all alcohol and drug rehabilitation failures in accordance with paragraph 1-
7d(4).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-30. Commanders of companies, detachments, and equivalent units
chunks.text:
The commander or designee will attend all Battalion At-Risk meetings with Behavioral Health and
SUD providers in order to maintain visibility of at-risk Soldiers. Ensure the URI is administered to all Sol-
diers at least 120 days before an operational deployment and the R - URI is administered to all Soldiers
between 30 and 180 days after returning from an operational deployment (see para 12 - 6 of this publica-
tion).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-31. Supervisors of Department of the Army Civilian employees
chunks.text: Consult with the Civilian Human Resources Service Center CHRSC employee relations specialist-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-31. Supervisors of Department of the Army Civilian employees
chunks.text: Before initiating any formal disciplinary or adverse action.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-31. Supervisors of Department of the Army Civilian employees
chunks.text: When an employee appears to be under the influence of alcohol or other drugs while on duty.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-31. Supervisors of Department of the Army Civilian employees
chunks.text: When an employee has been reported as an illegal drug user (verified positive drug test).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-31. Supervisors of Department of the Army Civilian employees
chunks.text:
