Each Army installation, ARNG, and USAR should prepare and maintain an annual fiscal year sum-
mary of the results of its DOT alcohol and other DTPs. The information required is found in 49 CFR
382.403.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-33. Department of Transportation reporting requirements
chunks.text:
Each installation ASAP manager will ensure that the Form DOT F 1385 (U.S. Department of Trans-
portation Drug and Alcohol Testing MIS Data Collection Form) is completed no later than the 28th of each
month. Test data are to be maintained for at least 5 years.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 5 > 5-33. Department of Transportation reporting requirements
chunks.text:
ASAP managers of installation, ARNG and USAR commands will support the annual summary report
by promptly forwarding the annual completed Form DOT F 1385 to the Director, DPRR no later than 1
March of each year. The Director, DPRR will summarize and analyze the data and forward a completed
report to the Department of Health and Human Services and to Office of the Secretary of Transportation,
Drug Enforcement and Program Compliance.

To assess and manage the program, the ASAP manager must collect, maintain, and analyze ASAP
statistics, but must also prevent the disclosure of personal information to unauthorized personnel. The
ASAP manager will use these statistics to:

Brief leaders about the status of their programs and highlight issues (for example, drug abuse
trends, testing rates, discrepancy rates, and so forth) that need commanders’ or supervisors’ attention.

Modify, as needed, the local ASAP training methods or channels for disseminating prevention infor-
mation and materials.
The DTC will maintain the following statistics:
Number of personnel tested under DOT rules.
Number of DOT UA specimens collected per reason for test.
Number of alcohol breath tests conducted per reason for test.
The DOT positive rates by drug.
The DOT discrepancy rate.
The DOT testing rate.

Department of the Army Civilian Employee Army Substance Abuse Program Services
This chapter specifies policies of the ASAP pertaining to DA Civilians. (See chapter 7 for DA Ci-
vilian eligibility for SUD treatment.) (Additional instructions and procedural guidance are pro-
vided in DA Pam 600 - 85.)

Substance abuse is inconsistent with the high standards of performance, discipline, and readiness
necessary to accomplish the Army’s mission. The Army’s goal is to provide a safe, healthful, productive,
and secure workplace.

DA Civilian employees should take a proactive approach when dealing with substance abuse or
other personal problems that affect their ability to perform their jobs or impact their well-being.

This chapter specifies policies of the ASAP pertaining to DA Civilian employees in appropriated and
non-appropriated fund positions. (Additional instructions and procedural guidance are provided in DA
Pam 600 - 85.)

Reducing or eliminating alcohol and/or drug misuse or abuse creates safe, healthful, productive, and
secure workplaces.

DA Civilian employees must refrain from alcohol abuse while on duty and must refrain from illegal
drug use. Substance abuse is inconsistent with the high standards of performance, discipline, and readi-
ness necessary to accomplish the Army’s mission.
All employees share the responsibility in reducing or eliminating substance abuse in the workplace.

Supervisors will consult early with CHRSC when suspected problematic substance use, or other re-
lated problems may adversely affect an employee’s job performance, behavior, and conduct in the work-
place.

Supervisors should consult with the EAPC, who helps employees with problems that may affect their
job performance, attendance, and/or conduct. (EAP procedures are provided in DA Pam 600 - 85.)

DA Civilian employees eligible to receive EAP services will receive screening/assessment and/or re-
ferral services for treatment of problematic substance use and personal problems that may affect their job
performance and/or well-being.
The SUDCC services will be offered to those eligible for healthcare in the military healthcare system.

Civilian employees may receive care at the MTF, if eligible, or a referral for SUD services will be pro-
vided.

An employee’s Family may be involved in treatment if appropriate and if the employee agrees to and
signs releases of information for such involvement.

DA Civilian employees may be granted leave or approved absence to obtain evaluation and treatment
in accordance with existing civilian personnel regulations and local union agreements.
Performance plans will not mention current or past treatment for SUD.

For further DoD EAP guidance regarding the DoD civilian employee Drug-Free Workplace Program
refer to DoDI 1010.09.

Adolescent Family members of Soldier and civilian employees in Outside Continental United States
(OCONUS) locations will be provided adolescent support and counseling services (ASACS) through a
DoDEA school.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-3. Purpose of the Employee Assistance Program
chunks.text:
The Army’s EAP includes a wide variety of services to promote productivity, reduce absenteeism, en-
hance job performance and/or wellbeing, and support worksite safety, in order to ensure the Army’s mis-
sion is accomplished in an efficient manner.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-3. Purpose of the Employee Assistance Program
chunks.text:
EAP services include, but are not limited to, screening/assessment, short-term counseling, and refer-
ral for problems that may affect job performance and/or wellbeing (see DoDI 1010.09 for further guid-
ance).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-3. Purpose of the Employee Assistance Program
chunks.text:
EAP services offer employees and supervisors guidance, consultation, mediation, and prevention
education which promotes employee productivity and wellbeing. Supervisory services of consultation and
mediation are provided to guide employees and managers in resolving issues that may impact the
productivity of the civilian workforce.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-3. Purpose of the Employee Assistance Program
chunks.text:
Referral or short-term counseling assistance to Family members of DA Civilian employees is pro-
vided to help resolve personal concerns and enhance the employee’s ability to perform their work duties.

Supervisors and management will refer DA Civilians employees to the EAP whose job performance, con-
duct, or attendance records may be indicative of personal problems requiring professional assistance.

EAP services are available on a voluntary basis for all eligible populations served, to include DA Ci-
vilian employees referred by supervisors due to job performance, conduct, or other work-related issues,
such as a supervisor’s suspicion that the employee is abusing drugs or alcohol. Refer to paragraphs 3 - 10
and 10 - 31 for special circumstances involving employees who are found to have used illegal drugs or to
be impaired by alcohol while on duty.

With certain exceptions related to illegal drug use, supervisors will not use the employee’s refusal of
EAP assistance, or the outcome of services rendered as the basis for taking disciplinary action. For ex-
ceptions, refer to section III of chapter 3 and paragraph 10 - 31.

Supervisors must base their disciplinary decisions and actions on the employee’s job performance
and/or conduct issues.

Supervisors should inform all DA Civilian employees who display performance and/or conduct issues
that the EAP may help them address personal problems that may have the potential to affect perfor-
mance and conduct.

Supervisors will promote EAP services as a benefit of employment. These services are not limited to
worksite-related problems. (See DA Pam 600 - 85 for evaluation and referral procedures by the EAPC.)

Family members may participate in all aspects of the ASAP, except drug testing, within capabilities of ex-
isting resources and to the extent they are eligible under paragraph 1 - 9. Adolescent Family members of
Soldiers and civilian employees in OCONUS locations will be provided by ASACS as directed in para-
graph 6-2m. ASACS are provided in OCONUS DoD and designated schools with counselors available
and located in these schools. Administrators, teachers, family members and students are made aware of
services during student orientations and school handbooks.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-6. Conflict of interest-Employee Assistance Program coordinator and civilian drug testing
chunks.text:
issues
At installations where the EAPC is a separate position from other drug testing roles, EAPCs will not take
part in the selection or collection process of civilian employee testing in support of the DFW to include
DOT testing or the reporting of test results. It is a conflict of interest for the EAPC to conduct these activi-
ties or to have the ability to determine testing dates for affected civilian employees. At installations where
the EAPC is combined with other drug testing roles, manager, DTC, and so forth, the ASAP will develop a
mechanism whereby an SJA-approved neutral witness observes the selection method in accordance with
DHHS guidelines for random civilian drug testing. The neutral witness must not be part of the testing pool
to ensure neutrality. EAPCs will inform TDPs of the provision of paragraph 6-7c at the initial intake ses-
sion when reviewing the limits of confidentiality.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-7. Confidentiality of civilian client records and information
chunks.text:
The confidentiality and disclosure of records of the identity, diagnosis, prognosis, prevention, or re-
habilitation of any client maintained in connection with a federal substance abuse program is controlled by
42 USC 290dd-2 and 42 CFR Part 2. Generally, disclosure of such records is prohibited except under the
following circumstances:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-7. Confidentiality of civilian client records and information
chunks.text: The client has consented in writing in accordance with 42 CFR Part 2, Subpart C.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-7. Confidentiality of civilian client records and information
chunks.text:
Records are released to medical personnel to the extent necessary to meet a bona fide medical
emergency.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-7. Confidentiality of civilian client records and information
chunks.text:
Records are released to qualified personnel for the purpose of conducting scientific research, man-
agement audits, financial audits, or program evaluation. But such personnel may not identify, directly or
indirectly, any individual patient in any report of such research, audit, or evaluation, or otherwise disclose
patient identities in any manner.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-7. Confidentiality of civilian client records and information
chunks.text: If authorized by an appropriate order of a court of competent jurisdiction.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-7. Confidentiality of civilian client records and information
chunks.text:
An employee does not have to be enrolled in the program in order to be protected by the provisions
of 42 USC 290dd-2 and 42 CFR Part 2, as long as the employee is considered a “patient”. A “patient” is
defined in 42 CFR 2.11 as “any individual who has applied for or been given diagnosis or treatment for
alcohol or drug abuse at federally assisted program.” The act of requesting an assisted program for an
alcohol or drug abuse problem places the individual under the protection of these laws.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-7. Confidentiality of civilian client records and information
chunks.text:
The confidential nature of counseling records of civilian employees with alcohol or other drug prob-
lems will be preserved according to applicable laws, rules, and regulations. In situations where a TDP
employee discloses to the EAPC the current use of illegal drugs or significant alcohol abuse, and the em-
ployee has not given written permission to disclose the information, the EAPC must consult with the in-
stallation ASAP manager and the servicing legal office without releasing identifying information of the
TDP employee for guidance regarding disclosure to the supervisory chain for purposes of determining
temporary abeyance of TDP duties.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-7. Confidentiality of civilian client records and information
chunks.text:
During the initial encounter with EAP the client will be notified of the Federal confidentiality require-
ments and will be given a written summary of the Federal laws and regulations. A sample notice can be
found in 42 CFR 2.22.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-7. Confidentiality of civilian client records and information
chunks.text:
Clients may have access to their own records, including an opportunity to inspect and copy any rec-
ords that the program maintains about the client. A client’s written request for such access, although not
required to obtain records, is encouraged.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-7. Confidentiality of civilian client records and information
chunks.text:
Civilian EAP and SUD records will be maintained in accordance with 42 CFR 2.16; 49 CFR Part 382;
AR 25 - 400 - 2; and the EAP Coordinator Guidebook. EAP files will be maintained at the ASAP for a pe-
riod of 5 years after file closure in accordance with the ARIMS under Record Retention Number RN 600-
85a2.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-7. Confidentiality of civilian client records and information
chunks.text:
The Privacy Act of 1974 (As Amended) (5 USC 552a) also applies to all information maintained in a
system of records retrievable to an employee’s name or other personal identifiers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-7. Confidentiality of civilian client records and information
chunks.text:
SUD counseling records of any civilian will meet the requirements of AR 40 - 66/DHA - AI 6025.26
and the HIPAA.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-7. Confidentiality of civilian client records and information
chunks.text:
EAP files will be maintained and secured (for example, in a secure room, locked file cabinet, or safe)
separate from other administrative files.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-8. Confidentiality of alcohol and drug test results
chunks.text:
Release of alcohol and/or drug test results is governed by provisions of The Privacy Act of 1974 (As
Amended) (5 USC 552a), and DOT regulations. PL 100 - 71, Section 503 (e) (5 USC 7301 note) further
restricts the release of drug test results.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-8. Confidentiality of alcohol and drug test results
chunks.text:
The results of a drug test of a civilian employee may not be disclosed without prior written consent of
the employee, unless the disclosure would be-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-8. Confidentiality of alcohol and drug test results
chunks.text: To the employee’s MRO.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-8. Confidentiality of alcohol and drug test results
chunks.text:
To the administrator of any EAP in which the employee is receiving counseling or treatment or is
otherwise participating.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-8. Confidentiality of alcohol and drug test results
chunks.text:
To any supervisory or management official in the employee’s agency having authority to take ad-
verse personnel action against the employee.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-8. Confidentiality of alcohol and drug test results
chunks.text:
Pursuant to the order of a court of competent jurisdiction where required by the Government to de-
fend against any adverse personnel action.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-8. Confidentiality of alcohol and drug test results
chunks.text: The FTDTL will release drug test results only to the MRO.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-8. Confidentiality of alcohol and drug test results
chunks.text:
Alcohol and other drug test results may be released to appropriate Army personnel for data collec-
tion and other purposes consistent with PL 100 - 71, Section 503(f); DOT regulations on controlled sub-
stances and alcohol abuse and testing; the DHHS Mandatory Guidelines for Federal Workplace DTPs;
and other DA requirements. The disclosure may not include personal identifying information on any em-
ployee.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-8. Confidentiality of alcohol and drug test results
chunks.text:
In accordance with DOT regulations, employees subject to DOT regulations are entitled, upon written
request, to copies of and access to records relating to the employee’s use of alcohol or controlled sub-
stances, including records pertaining to their alcohol and controlled substance abuse test.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 6 > 6-8. Confidentiality of alcohol and drug test results
chunks.text:
In accordance with PL 100 - 71, Section 503, Federal employees are entitled, upon written request, to
have access to any records pertaining to their test and any records relating to the results of any relevant
laboratory certification, review, or revocation of certification proceeding.

Identification, Referral for Treatment
Generally, this chapter applies to Soldiers. It applies to DA Civilian employees and Family mem-
bers where specifically noted.

The Army recognizes that SUDs are often preventable and treatable. While self-referral is preferred, com-
manders are also responsible for identifying Soldiers at risk for a SUD and for referring them to the BH
clinic for a SUD evaluation and actively supporting intervention and treatment as clinically indicated. SUD
treatment determinations, including level of care needed, will be made by the treating provider for the
SUD treatment services.

Alcohol. Soldiers with alcohol-related discipline events (for example, DUI, assault, UCMJ) or those
identified by the commander as abusing alcohol will be referred to BH for a SUD evaluation.

Other Substances. All Soldiers, to include ARNG and USAR Soldiers ordered to AD under Title 10,
who are identified as having unauthorized use of substances or suspected substance abuse, will be re-
ferred to the BH clinic for a SUD evaluation.
Early ID is a critical aspect of the SUD evaluation process and occurs through a variety of methods-
Voluntary (self).
Command.
Drug testing.
Alcohol testing.
Medical.
Investigation/apprehension.
Other, for example FAP.

Commands will identify Soldiers at risk for having a SUD based upon evidence provided by these
methods.

Voluntary (self) ID is the most desirable method of identifying SUD. The individual whose perfor-
mance, social conduct, interpersonal relations, or health becomes impaired because of these problems
has the personal obligation to seek help. The Soldier’s unit commander must become involved in support-
ing voluntary help seeking and early ID of problematic substance use. Command policies will encourage
Soldiers and DA Civilian employees to voluntarily seek assistance and will avoid actions that would dis-
courage individuals from seeking help. Soldiers may initially request help from their commander, primary
care provider, chaplain, any officer or NCO in their chain of command, or other agencies. Soldiers seek-
ing self-referral for problematic substance use may access services through BH services for a SUD evalu-
ation. The Limited Use Policy exists to encourage Soldiers to proactively seek help.

In situations where a Soldier reveals to a chaplain or chaplain assistant that they have or have had
an alcohol or other drug use problem, confidential communication could limit a chaplain from notifying a
Soldier’s unit commander. However, the Soldier may waive the communication privilege and allow the
chaplain to inform the unit commander. If the Soldier does not waive their privilege, the chaplain will in-
form the Soldier that treatment for a SUD is available through the BH clinic.

ID resulting from a Soldier seeking emergency treatment for an actual or possible alcohol or other
drug overdose, not subsequent to a traffic accident or criminal offense, is considered to be a variation of
voluntary (self) ID, and therefore, the limited use policy will apply. For reporting purposes, such cases will
be classified as self-referral.

Civilian employees will be offered screening/assessment, short-term counseling and referral for SUD
treatment services, if eligible to receive care at the MTF, or to rehabilitation/treatment programs off the
installation (see chapter 6). Supervisors will follow procedures outlined in DA Pam 600 - 85.

Military Family members will receive SUD treatment services within their assigned MTF; if space is
not available, a referral to the network for appropriate level of care will be given.

Commander/supervisor ID occurs when a commander/supervisor observes, suspects, or otherwise
becomes aware of an individual whose job performance, social conduct, interpersonal relations, physical
fitness, or health appears to be affected adversely by suspected problematic substance use. Soldiers who
are identified with possible problematic substance use will be processed by their unit commander or des-
ignated representative in accordance with AR 600 - 85 using DA Form 8003 and referred to the BH clinic
for a SUD evaluation. All referrals made with a DA Form 8003 will have a SUD evaluation performed by
the BH clinic. If mandated treatment for SUD is required, the recommended frequency, length of counsel-
ing sessions, and level of treatment will be shared with the commander to solicit support.

Drug testing ID is accomplished through UA, which is discussed in detail in chapter 4 for Soldiers
and in chapter 5 for civilian employees.

Any Soldier identified with a verified drug positive requires a mandatory command referral to the BH
clinic for a SUD evaluation within 5 duty days of receipt of the validated positive drug test results. The
commander must also refer the Soldier to ADAPT within 5 duty days of notification. When this identifica-
tion method applies, the commander will describe the Limited Use Policy to the Soldier. The DA Form
8003 will be marked with reason for command referral as biochemical.

Any civilian employee identified with a verified drug positive requires a mandatory referral to the EAP
for an assessment.

Alcohol testing identification is accomplished through alcohol breathalyzer or blood sample alcohol
testing which is discussed in chapter 3 of this regulation.

Any Soldier on duty whose alcohol breathalyzer or blood sample alcohol test result indicates alcohol
impairment as discussed in paragraph 3 - 2 requires a mandatory command referral to ADAPT and also
the BH clinic for a SUD evaluation within 5 duty days of receipt of the test result.

When this identification method applies, the commander will describe the Limited Use Policy to the
Soldier and refer the Soldier to the BH clinic for a SUD evaluation using the DA Form 8003 form as soon
as possible. The DA Form 8003 will be marked with reason for referral as biochemical.

Any civilian employee subject to the DOT breath testing for employees performing duties requiring a
commercial driver’s license will require a mandatory referral to the ASAP EAP for screening/assessment
and referral to the installation SUDCC if the confirmed alcohol test result is 0.04 percent or higher. Super-
visors will follow procedures outlined in DA Pam 600 - 85, if confirmed alcohol test is 0.02 percent or
higher.

A Soldier‘s problematic substance use may be identified through military or civilian law enforcement
investigation and/or apprehension. The unit commander will refer the Soldier to ADAPT and a mandatory
command referral to the BH clinic for a SUD evaluation within 5 duty days of notification of apprehension.
Referral form, DA Form 8003, will be marked with the reason for referral as investigation/apprehension.
Referral for evaluation or treatment does not interfere with or preclude pending legal or administrative ac-
tions.

When this identification method applies, the commander will describe the Limited Use Policy to the
Soldier. and refer the Soldier to the BH clinic for a SUD evaluation using the DA Form 8003 as soon as
possible. The DA Form 8003 will be marked with reason for referral as investigation/apprehension.

During routine or emergency medical treatment, health care providers may note problematic sub-
stance use. In such instances, health care providers will refer the Soldier to the BH clinic for a SUD evalu-
ation. If a Soldier reveals abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled drug, alcohol or other) to a health
care provider, the health care provider will refer to BH for a SUD evaluation, if necessary. The revelation
of problematic substance use, by itself, will not subject the individual to adverse administrative action. UA
which may follow such disclosure will be covered under the Limited Use Policy. When this identification
method applies, the commander will describe the Limited Use Policy to the Soldier as soon as possible
and support the medical referral of the Soldier to the BH clinic for a SUD evaluation. The health care pro-
vider will provide information about the Soldier’s alleged problematic substance use immediately to the
Soldier’s commander, should it appear that any of the following conditions exist:
The Soldier is acutely impaired.
Impaired judgment is evident.

Potential danger to others exists as a result of problematic substance use (for example, Chemical or
Nuclear Surety Programs, flight crews).

Problematic substance use impacts the Soldier’s judgment, reliability, or trustworthiness to protect
classified information.

If a health care provider notes problematic substance use during routine or emergency medical
screening of a civilian employee or Family member, the health care provider will recommend the individ-
ual for referral to BH for a SUD evaluation.

The evaluation, identification, and referral of health care providers with abuse of substances (illegal
drug, controlled drug, alcohol or other) related problems are very sensitive issues. Health care providers
are responsible for helping to identify and refer any colleague whose performance is impaired by alcohol
or other drugs to the Impaired Health Care Provider Program. All health care providers will be responsible
for reporting any problematic substance use concerns to the Impaired Health Care Provider Committee or
chain of command. The medical health authority will manage the potentially impaired provider through the
Impaired Health Care Provider Committee. When this identification method applies, the medical com-
mander will describe the Limited Use Policy to the healthcare provider as soon as possible and support
the medical referral of the Soldier to the BH clinic for a SUD evaluation.

Types of Substance Use Disorder Treatment
The procedures described below are meant to assist commanders in distinguishing between
mandatory enrolled substance abuse treatment and voluntary alcohol-related treatment by be-
havior healthcare. Soldiers who meet the criteria in paragraph 8-2a are required to enroll and
participate in mandatory SUD treatment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 8 > 8-1. Types of Substance Use Disorder Treatment Services
chunks.text:
Failure to progress in mandatory treatment continues to serve as a basis for administrative separa-
tion. The evaluation for mandatory treatment must include collateral information, as appropriate, and in-
volve command consultation as permitted by DoDI 1010.04 to support the Soldier’s treatment needs, in-
cluding level of care.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 8 > 8-1. Types of Substance Use Disorder Treatment Services
chunks.text:
Voluntary Alcohol-Related Behavioral Health Care. Soldiers who do not meet the criteria for manda-
tory enrolled SUD treatment described in paragraph 8-2b may voluntarily receive alcohol-related behav-
ioral healthcare without enrollment in mandatory substance abuse treatment. Before the start of voluntary
alcohol-related behavioral healthcare, Soldiers will be informed of the criteria for command notification
and enrollment in mandatory SUD treatment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 8 > 8-2. Criteria of Substance Use Disorder Treatment Services
chunks.text:
Criteria for Mandatory substance use disorder treatment. Behavioral health providers will conduct a
complete clinical assessment to determine that a SUD diagnosis is present. The Soldier will be enrolled in
mandatory SUD treatment, with notification to the Soldier’s command, when any of the following condi-
tions are present:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 8 > 8-2. Criteria of Substance Use Disorder Treatment Services
chunks.text:
The Soldier has a SUD diagnosis related to illegal drug use, including illegal use of prescription
drugs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 8 > 8-2. Criteria of Substance Use Disorder Treatment Services
chunks.text:
The Soldier’s abuse of substance (alcohol) is identified through military or civilian law enforcement
investigation and/or apprehension, and/or an alcohol breath or blood test indicates alcohol impairment
while the Soldier is on duty, and a diagnostic assessment confirms the presence of a SUD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 8 > 8-2. Criteria of Substance Use Disorder Treatment Services
chunks.text:
The Soldier is receiving more extensive treatment than standard outpatient behavioral healthcare,
including an addiction medicine intensive outpatient program, a partial hospitalization program, an inpa-
tient program, or a residential treatment program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 8 > 8-2. Criteria of Substance Use Disorder Treatment Services
chunks.text:
The Soldier’s substance use affects his or her judgment, reliability, or trustworthiness, or presents a
clear risk to safety, security, occupational functioning, or mission. Providers will notify commanders of any
concerns related to safety and/or mission readiness consistent with command notification requirements
for other behavioral health conditions, in accordance with DoDI 6490.08. Commanders are responsible
for notifying their security managers when these conditions arise, in accordance with DoDI 1010.04 and
AR 380 - 67.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 8 > 8-2. Criteria of Substance Use Disorder Treatment Services
chunks.text:
Criteria of Voluntary Alcohol-Related Behavioral Health Care. Alcohol-related behavioral healthcare
will follow the procedures applicable to all other types of behavioral healthcare. The Soldier will be eligible
to receive voluntary alcohol-related behavioral healthcare when the behavioral health provider conducts a
complete clinical assessment and determines that an alcohol-related behavioral health condition is pre-
sent, but the Soldier does not meet any of the criteria for enrollment in mandatory SUD treatment de-
scribed in paragraph 2b. In addition, the following procedures and guidelines apply:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 8 > 8-2. Criteria of Substance Use Disorder Treatment Services
chunks.text:
The components of the behavioral healthcare treatment plan, such as frequency and type of visits,
will be tailored by the healthcare provider in collaboration with the Soldier and to meet the Soldier’s clini-
cal needs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 8 > 8-2. Criteria of Substance Use Disorder Treatment Services
chunks.text:
If at any time a Soldier receiving voluntary alcohol-related behavioral healthcare meets any of the
criteria for enrollment in mandatory SUD treatment as described in paragraph 8-2a, the provider will no-
tify the Soldier’s commander and the Soldier will be enrolled in mandatory SUD treatment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 8 > 8-2. Criteria of Substance Use Disorder Treatment Services
chunks.text:
Voluntary alcohol-related behavioral healthcare will not prevent subsequent placement in mandatory
SUD treatment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 8 > 8-2. Criteria of Substance Use Disorder Treatment Services
chunks.text:
Discontinuation of voluntary alcohol-related behavioral healthcare for any reason will not be consid-
ered a rehabilitation (treatment) failure. A history of voluntary alcohol-related behavioral healthcare, in-
cluding discontinuation of this care, cannot be used as a basis for administrative separation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 8 > 8-2. Criteria of Substance Use Disorder Treatment Services
chunks.text:
Personnel actions. Soldiers in any type of SUD care, whether voluntary or mandatory, are subject to
any personnel action their commander deems appropriate, except as noted in paragraph 8-2b(4).

Rehabilitation team meetings (RTMs) are required for all Soldiers enrolled in mandatory SUD treat-
ment and their commander or command representative’s attendance is required. Although an in-person
RTM is valuable in the treatment process, especially initially, a telephonic RTM is sufficient to satisfy this
requirement except for in the following circumstances:

Recommendation for referral to an Addiction Medicine Intensive Outpatient Program or a SUD Resi-
dential Treatment Facility.

If at any time a Soldier receiving voluntary care for a SUD meets any of the criteria for Mandatory
Care, the provider will notify the Soldier’s commander to initiate enrollment. This includes any concern
related to safety and/or mission readiness, consistent with command notification requirements for other
BH conditions.

Soldiers in voluntary care could benefit from commander or supervisor support or input on an infor-
mal basis and are encouraged, though not required, to include them in their treatment process.

Any re-assessment that results in changing the level of care from voluntary non-enrolled status to
mandatory enrolled status (for example, if a Soldier progresses to moderate or severe diagnosis or is in-
volved in a substance-related indiscipline incident).

Safety. The command must be directly notified if at any time a Soldier is determined to be a threat to
self or others.

Missed appointments. The clinical provider should immediately notify the commander or first ser-
geant when a Soldier “no-shows” for their appointment if in the “enrolled in mandatory treatment” cate-
gory.

Active intoxication or impairment. The command must be notified, and a command escort must be
requested to be sent to the MTF if at any time it is suspected that a Soldier is actively intoxicated or im-
paired.

Poor treatment adherence. The clinical provider should notify the command if a Soldier who is en-
rolled in mandatory treatment fails to substantially comply with their treatment plan. Treatment noncompli-
ance may be grounds for a separation under Chapter 9 of AR 635 - 200. This will be communicated to
commanders through a DA Form 3822 (Report of Mental Status Evaluation), which will include a brief
treatment synopsis. Officer separations will be processed in accordance with the provisions of AR
600 - 8 - 24 and AR 135 - 175.

The Army considers aftercare to be outpatient support and monitoring that occurs after the acute
phase of outpatient treatment. Aftercare seeks to sustain gains made.

Although aftercare should be offered to all Soldiers following acute care, it is only required for those
Soldiers in mandatory care who were given a SUD diagnosis.

The total period of mandatory care and aftercare should be no less than 12 months after the active
disorder has remitted.

Prevention, Education, and Training
Section I
General

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-1. Alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder prevention, education, and training
chunks.text: objectives

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-1. Alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder prevention, education, and training
chunks.text: The objectives of alcohol, other drug abuse and gambling disorder prevention are to:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-1. Alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder prevention, education, and training
chunks.text: Prevent, deter, and reduce alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-1. Alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder prevention, education, and training
chunks.text:
Provide commanders, Soldiers, DA Civilian employees and Family members with substance abuse
and gambling disorder prevention and awareness training as requested, to include at a minimum the fol-
lowing:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-1. Alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder prevention, education, and training
chunks.text: The ASAP policies and services.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-1. Alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder prevention, education, and training
chunks.text: Consequences of alcohol, other drug abuse and gambling disorder.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-1. Alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder prevention, education, and training
chunks.text:
Incompatibility of alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder with readiness, physical and
mental fitness, Army Values, and the Warrior Ethos.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-1. Alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder prevention, education, and training
chunks.text:
All professional staff (program specific staff, SUD, MROs, and UDLS) must train, sustain, and im-
prove their skills, proficiency, and professionalism through:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-1. Alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder prevention, education, and training
chunks.text: Initial education and training courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-1. Alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder prevention, education, and training
chunks.text: Certification courses.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-1. Alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder prevention, education, and training
chunks.text: Professional development training programs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-1. Alcohol, other drug abuse, and gambling disorder prevention, education, and training
chunks.text: Support and encouragement for the professional certification of PCs and EAPCs.

Prevention includes all measures taken to deter and reduce the abuse of substances (illegal drug,
controlled drug, alcohol or other) and gambling disorder to the lowest possible level.

Prevention efforts are targeted to the total force and tailored to diverse groups and integrated with
other mission-related efforts.

Prevention initiatives will emphasize cooperation and partnerships with the installation and local com-
munities. Soldiers are encouraged to get involved in the local civilian community efforts to prevent or re-
duce abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled drug, alcohol and other) and gambling disorder.

Education and training programs must include information on the effects and consequences of
abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled drug, alcohol or other) and gambling disorder. These pro-
grams must also include information describing abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled drug, alcohol
or other) and gambling disorder services that are available at the installation and/or community.

Alcohol de-glamorization is an essential element of the Army prevention program. Marketing and
promotion of practices which glamorize alcohol use are prohibited. All members of the military community
will be provided with the information needed to make responsible decisions about personal use of alcohol.

Total Force is provided information to identify abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled drug, alco-
hol or other) or gambling disorder. Commanders and supervisors are provided tools and skills to enable
early identification of signs and symptoms of these problems.

Education on abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled drug, alcohol or other) or gambling disor-
der will be conducted throughout the Army Training System.

Education on abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled drug, alcohol or other) or gambling disor-
der will be compatible with the indoctrination of recruits in the standards of discipline, performance, and
behavior.

Leaders will support prevention efforts on abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled drug, alcohol
or other) or gambling disorder to maintain readiness.

ARD will develop prevention products for the Total Force installation. Training products will be up-
dated periodically.
Section II
Army Substance Abuse Program, Substance Use Disorder Clinical Care Staff and Unit Deterrence
Leader Training, Professional Development, and Certification

chunks.text: SUD counselors will receive training on-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-4. Department of the Army Training of Substance Use Disorder Clinical Care Staff

Current trends and practices in the identification, assessment, and referral of personnel at risk for

The Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test, Alcohol Consumption (AUDIT - C) training as a

chunks.text: Professional and Army certifications-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
Establish a minimum level of competency for quality service provided by ASAP staff members and
UDLs.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
Assure professional development for PCs, RRPCs, EAPC. Prevention certification is recommended

chunks.text: Give professional recognition to assigned positions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
Army Substance Abuse Program (ASAP) Managers. The ASAP Manager will attend the ASAP Pro-
gram Manager course within the first year of assuming the ASAP Manager duties and must complete the
refresher course every 3 years thereafter.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
Newly hired EAPCs must attain CEAP status through the Employee Assistance Certification Com-
mission established by the Employee Assistance Professionals Association within 3 years of assuming
their duties.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
This requirement will be written into the employee’s job description and be a condition of employ-
ment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
Individuals will be responsible to apply for certification and training and for maintaining all profes-
sional development requirements once they are certified. This requirement will be clearly posted in all va-
cancy announcements for EAPC positions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
EAPCs who fail to obtain their certification within 3 years of starting in that position or who fail to
maintain their EAP certification may be subject to administrative action and/or removal from these posi-
tions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text: ASAP Managers are encouraged to gain CEAP certification status.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
The EAPCs, who transfer to another installation and are hired as an EAPC with no break in EAPC
service, are bound by the EAPC certification start date at their first installation. The requirement to obtain
certification within 3 years from the date of employment at the first installation will remain in effect.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
The DTCs will be free of administrative proceedings and must not have had a drug or alcohol-related
incident within the last 3 years. DTCs who are not certified must work under the daily direct supervision of
a certified DTC. Using noncertified DTCs jeopardizes the credibility of the Army’s DTP. If the installation
or command does not have a certified DTC, UDLs will ship their units’ specimens directly to the FTDTL
for testing or the ASAP manager may request an exception to policy from the Director, DPRR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
Primary and alternate DTCs must be certified through the DA DTC Certification Course within 9
months of assuming their duties.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text: Primary and alternate DTCs must be recertified every 3 years.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
The requirement to obtain and maintain DA DTC certification will be written into the employee’s job
description and be a condition of employment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
DTCs who fail to obtain their certification or fail to maintain their certification may be subject to ad-
ministrative actions and removal from these positions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text: The DTCs should attend a course of instruction that teaches proper instructional methods and skills.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text: The ASAP managers should gain and maintain DTC certification.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
Additional personnel working in the Drug Test Collection Point that are not the primary or alternate
DTCs will have documented training by a certified DTC and be under the direct supervision of that DTC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-5. Army Substance Abuse Program staff training certifications
chunks.text:
An ASAP manager may temporarily restrict a DTC from handling UA specimens because of an al-
cohol or drug-related incident or pending legal or administrative proceedings until a final determination
has been made. Where the DTC is a civilian employee, the ASAP manager must first coordinate with the
servicing CHRSC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
UPL certification is crucial to the Army’s DTP and unit substance abuse prevention efforts. All UDLs, re-
gardless of component, must receive the same standardized curriculum and be certified to perform their
duties. The BDL qualifications, training, and certification are the same as those for UDLs; where UDL is
used in this paragraph, it applies to both UDLs and BDLs, unless otherwise stated.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text: Qualifications-military personnel.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
Be an officer, warrant officer, or NCO (E - 5 or above for UDL, E - 5 promotable or above for BDL)
(recommend E - 7 or above at all levels).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text: Be designated on appointment orders by the unit commander.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
Successfully complete DPRR’s standardized UDL- CTP prior to collecting any drug testing speci-
mens.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text: Not currently enrolled in mandatory SUD treatment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
Not be under investigation for legal, administrative, or abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled
drug, alcohol or other) related offenses or incident within the last 3 years. Soldiers that have previously
been in SUD treatment or completion of ADAPT should not be considered as potential UDLs for at least
36 months after release from counseling or completion of ADAPT.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
Commanders should request a local review of the UDL candidate’s medical, personnel, and criminal
records and a background check by the garrison/local ASAP for past drug positive UA test and by the
MTF BH clinic for past enrollment in mandatory alcohol or drug treatment. The commander will make the
final decision to appoint the candidate based on all the information received except that the requirements
in paragraphs 9-6a (1) through 9-6a(5), above, are not waiverable.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text: Qualifications-Department of the Army Civilian employees.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
If military personnel are not reasonably and consistently available to perform UDL duties, those UDL
duties may be performed by a civilian employee, providing all of the following criteria are met:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text: The employee must be, at minimum, a GS - 5 or pay grade equivalent.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
The employee must be trained and certified as a UDL in accordance with AR 600 - 85, chapter 2 re-
quirements and must be recertified every 24 months.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text: The UDL duties must be annotated in the employee’s position description.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
Trained and certified DTCs can serve as UDLs in accordance with the criteria set forth in paragraph
9-6b(1).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
Certification. The UDLs must be certified to perform their duties by successfully completing the DA
UDL CTP, a standardized course of instruction and evaluation. No other UDL certification course is au-
thorized without the written approval of the Director, DPRR. If a UDL candidate is deployed, they may be
certified using the distance learning and certification procedures explained at www.armyresili-
ence.army.mil. Upon successful completion of all course requirements, UDLs will receive a certificate of
training and a UDL certification card. A UDL that is reassigned to another command may be appointed as
a UPL in the new command with proof of a previous certification until recertification is required at the 18-
month point.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text: Recertification.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
UDLs must be recertified every 24 months by successfully completing the UDL CTP exam. The
ASAP and unit commander will initiate a new local and DAMIS background check to ensure that the UDL
is still qualified in accordance with paragraphs 9-6a(1) through (6), above.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
If a UDL’s certification expires, the UDL has up to 60 days to contact the ASAP to attend any lo-
cally-required update training, take and pass the recertification exam to be recertified for another 24
months from the date of examination. During the time between the expiration date and the exam the UPL
is not authorized to collect drug testing specimens. If a UDL’s certification has been expired for more than
60 days, then the UDL must retake the entire UPL certification course.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
The ASAP managers may revoke the ASAP certification of any UDL for an excessive number of dis-
crepancies in drug testing collection procedures, UA specimens, or on associated forms. However, the
ASAP manager must immediately notify the UDL’s commander, in writing, of such revocation and the pur-
pose for it.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-6. Battalion/Unit Deterrence Leader qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
Online certification. The online CTP for certification and recertification of deployed Soldiers is only
valid for 12 months. Upon redeployment, the UDL must contact the home station ASAP before conducting
any collections.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-7. Drug testing coordinator qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
The DTC certification is crucial to the Army’s DTP and substance abuse prevention efforts. All DTCs must
receive the same standardized curriculum and be certified to perform their duties. On installations, there
is usually a DTC and an alternate DTC; however, other personnel who are not DTC-certified may also
serve as DTCs as long as they meet the requirements specified below:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-7. Drug testing coordinator qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text: Qualification s.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-7. Drug testing coordinator qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
DA Civilian employee (certified DTC or GS - 05 or equivalent and above), officer, warrant officer, or
NCO (E - 5 or above).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-7. Drug testing coordinator qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text: Designated on appointment orders by the senior commander/manager.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-7. Drug testing coordinator qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text: Successfully complete the DPRR standardized CTP prior to collecting any drug testing specimens.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-7. Drug testing coordinator qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text: Not be currently enrolled/mandatory care in the SUD treatment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-7. Drug testing coordinator qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
Not have a conviction or other adverse finding, or be under investigation, for abuse of a substance
(illegal drug, controlled drug, alcohol or other) offense or misconduct when the underlying facts occurred
within the last 3 years.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-7. Drug testing coordinator qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
Certification. DTCs must be certified to perform their duties by successfully completing either the DA
DTC Certification Course or the DA DTC CTP, a standardized course of instruction and evaluation. No
other DTC certification courses are authorized without the written approval of the Director, DPRR. If a
DTC candidate is deployed, they may be certified using the distance learning and certification procedures
explained at https://armyresilience.army.mil. Upon successful completion of all course requirements,
DTCs will receive a certificate of training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-7. Drug testing coordinator qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text: Recertification.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-7. Drug testing coordinator qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
The DTCs must recertify every 3 years by successfully completing the DTC CTP exam. If a DTC’s
certification expires while they are deployed, the DTC may recertify using the distance learning and certifi-
cation procedures at para 9-6b. If a DTC fails the re-certification exam, they must retake the entire DTC
CTP before retaking the exam.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-7. Drug testing coordinator qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
If a DTC’s certification expires, the DTC has up to 90 days to contact the ASAP to attend any lo-
cally-required update training, take and pass the recertification exam to be recertified for another year
from the date of examination. During the time between the expiration date and the exam the DTC is not
authorized to collect drug testing specimens. If a DTC’s certification has been expired for more than 90
days, then the DTC must retake the entire DTC certification course.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-7. Drug testing coordinator qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text:
The ASAP managers may revoke the certification of any DTC for an excessive number of discrep-
ancies in drug testing collection procedures, UA specimens, or on associated forms. However, if the DTC
is military, the ASAP manager must immediately notify the DTC’s commander in writing of such revoca-
tion and the reasons for it.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-7. Drug testing coordinator qualifications, training, and certification
chunks.text: Random pool. The DTCs are encouraged to volunteer to be added to the random drug testing pool.

Be a DA Civilian employee (GS - 05 or equivalent and above), officer, warrant officer or NCO (E - 5
or above).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-8. Department of Transportation Drug Test Collector, screening test technician, and installation
chunks.text: Be designated on appointment orders by the ASAP manager.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-8. Department of Transportation Drug Test Collector, screening test technician, and installation
chunks.text: Successfully complete the DPRR standardized CTP prior to collecting any drug testing specimens.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-8. Department of Transportation Drug Test Collector, screening test technician, and installation
chunks.text:
Not currently enrolled in mandatory SUD treatment, if using a Soldier or in substance abuse rehabil-
itation, if using a civilian.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-8. Department of Transportation Drug Test Collector, screening test technician, and installation
chunks.text:
Not have a conviction or other adverse finding, or be under investigation for abuse of substance (il-
legal drug, controlled drug, alcohol or other) related offenses or misconduct when the underlying facts oc-
curred within the last 3 years.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-8. Department of Transportation Drug Test Collector, screening test technician, and installation
chunks.text:
Certification. DOT Drug Test Collectors must be certified to perform their duties by successfully
completing the DA DOT Drug Test Collector CTP, a standardized course of instruction and evaluation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-8. Department of Transportation Drug Test Collector, screening test technician, and installation
chunks.text:
Recertification. DOT Drug Test Collectors must recertify every 5 years by successfully completing
the current DA DOT Drug Test Collector CTP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-8. Department of Transportation Drug Test Collector, screening test technician, and installation
chunks.text: Error Correction Training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-8. Department of Transportation Drug Test Collector, screening test technician, and installation
chunks.text:
A DOT Drug Test Collector should receive error correction training within 30 days of being notified
of making an error in the collection process that causes a collection to be cancelled or makes the speci-
men untestable. Error correction training is explained at 49 CFR, Part 40, Subpart C. If the collector does
not complete error correction training within 30 days of notification, the collector is no longer authorized to
conduct DOT collections until the training is completed. Error correction training must be administered by
a qualified collector as explained in 49 CFR, Part 40, Subpart C. The qualified collector, who conducts the
error correction training, must attest in writing that the training was completed, and the mock collections
were error free. The supervisor of the collector receiving the error correction training will review and retain
this document for 3 years.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-8. Department of Transportation Drug Test Collector, screening test technician, and installation
chunks.text:
The ASAP managers may revoke the certification of any DOT Drug Test Collector for an excessive
number of discrepancies in drug testing collection procedures, UA specimens, or on associated forms.
However, if the DOT Drug Test Collector is military, the ASAP manager must immediately notify their
commander in writing of such revocation and the reasons for it.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-8. Department of Transportation Drug Test Collector, screening test technician, and installation
chunks.text:
STT and installation BAT. STTs and installation BATs must meet the qualification training require-
ments of 49 CFR, Part 40, Subpart J prior to collecting any specimens for DOT alcohol tests. Refresher
training and error correction training requirements are also described in this Subpart.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-8. Department of Transportation Drug Test Collector, screening test technician, and installation
chunks.text:
The DOT drug test collectors, STTs, and installation BATs are encouraged to volunteer to be added
to the random drug testing pool.
Section III
Education and Training Requirements

The DPRR and ASAPs will provide training during pre-deployment and redeployment on the topics of
abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled drug, alcohol or other) and gambling disorder awareness.

Commanders of all components will ensure that they deploy with at least two certified UDLs. The
commander will ensure UDLs receive specialized pre-deployment training, supplies, and other special in-
structions from the ASAP staff prior to deployment.

TRADOC will ensure training on the topics of abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled drug, alco-
hol or other) and gambling disorder awareness is conducted during initial entry, pre-commissioning and is
also integrated into all other Army professional development courses.

All ASAP curriculum developed for TRADOC schools/courses will be reviewed and approved by the
Director, ARD.

The DPRR and AMEDDC&S staffs will be available to provide training at senior leadership training
courses upon request.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-11. Soldier substance abuse and gambling disorder awareness training
chunks.text:
All newly assigned Soldiers will receive a newcomers briefing by the commander or designated rep-
resentative within 30 days of reporting. At a minimum the briefing will provide information on ASAP ser-
vices, the location of ASAP services, community laws, command policies, drug and alcohol free activities
and the Limited Use Policy.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-11. Soldier substance abuse and gambling disorder awareness training
chunks.text:
Corporals and above will receive information in professional military education from formal education
institutions and from installation prevention coordinators as requested by Commanders, on the signs and
symptoms of on the topics of abuse of substances (illegal drug, controlled drug, alcohol or other) and
gambling disorder and how to refer to BH for evaluation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-11. Soldier substance abuse and gambling disorder awareness training
chunks.text:
All Soldiers, commanders and leaders, to include ARNG and USAR Soldiers on AD/in Title 10 status,
will receive annual alcohol and drug misuse prevention training.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-11. Soldier substance abuse and gambling disorder awareness training
chunks.text:
Commanders and Leaders’ (Squad Leaders and above) annual substance misuse prevention train-
ing will consist of how to identify, assess, and refer personnel displaying signs of problematic alcohol and
substance use as well as the various services that are available for treatment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-11. Soldier substance abuse and gambling disorder awareness training
chunks.text:
Annual alcohol and substance misuse prevention training will be conducted face-to-face. Command-
ers will determine the duration, location and means for conducting training, and implement risk reduction
measures based on unit assessments. Unit leaders will lead the training and may use SMEs as available
and needed.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-11. Soldier substance abuse and gambling disorder awareness training
chunks.text:
All unit training whether conducted by the commander, the ASAP staff, or a guest speaker will be
documented using a sign-in sheet to record who attended, the topic, the date, start time, and end time of
the class. A copy of the sign-in sheet will be provided to the installation ASAP staff within 5 working days.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text: ASAP services to include the EAP.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text: The location and hours of operation of ASAP services.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text: Community laws.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text: Command policies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text: Confidentiality.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text: When they are subject to drug testing, including reasonable suspicion and post-accident testing.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text:
Employees in TDPs and employees subject to DOT drug and alcohol testing will receive information
on selection methods and testing procedures.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text:
The supervisors of employees subject to DOT drug and alcohol testing will receive the required
training outlined in 49 CFR 382.603.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text: All supervisors will receive training within 60 days of assuming supervisory duties.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text:
The supervisor’s role in the recognition and documentation of employee performance and conduct
problems, and the use of and responsibilities for offering EAP services.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text:
The supervisor’s responsibilities and procedures for notifying TDPs and DOT personnel of their se-
lection for testing.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text:
Availability of EAP services including the EAP POC, telephone number, address, and hours of oper-
ation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text: The process of reintegrating the employee after rehabilitation into the workforce.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text: Confidentiality and records requirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text:
The ASAP prevention, education, and training for civilian employees will include training provided in
conjunction with existing civilian personnel orientation and training programs. Civilian employees may re-
ceive additional alcohol, other drug and gambling disorder awareness training as mission needs dictates.
Employee education will address as required by position:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text:
ASAP policies, the Army DFW Civilian DTP, DOT Controlled Substances and Alcohol Abuse and
Testing regulations and requirements, and information on the availability of EAP services, to include the
EAP POC, telephone number, address, and hours of operation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text:
Types, effects, signs, and symptoms of substance abuse (illegal drug, controlled drug, alcohol or
other) and the hazards/effects on performance and conduct.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-12. Civilian employee substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text: Program confidentiality.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-13. Family member and K - 12 substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text:
The ASAP is encouraged to develop, support and/or sponsor anti-drug and alcohol abuse programs
for community K - 12 schools that are on, or formally associated with, the military installation. The ASAP
prevention education and training at community schools will be addressed in the annual prevention plan.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-13. Family member and K - 12 substance abuse awareness training
chunks.text:
The ASAP prevention education and training of Family members will be addressed as mission dic-
tates a need. Attendance by Family members, retirees, and their families will be on a voluntary basis.
Training will highlight the local laws, impact of abuse, availability of counseling, rehabilitation/treatment
services, and alternatives to abuse of illegal drug, controlled drug, alcohol or other products.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-14. Alcohol and other drug abuse prevention training
chunks.text:
The ADAPT is an educational/motivational intervention which focuses on the adverse effects and
consequences of problematic substance use. The ADAPT courses will consist of at least 12 hours of
course material.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-14. Alcohol and other drug abuse prevention training
chunks.text:
The DA-approved curricula for ADAPT will be approved by DPRR. Requests for exceptions to the
ADAPT curriculum or to conduct alternate curriculum research trials will be submitted in writing to the Di-
rector, DPRR for approval.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-14. Alcohol and other drug abuse prevention training
chunks.text: The following personnel will attend ADAPT:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-14. Alcohol and other drug abuse prevention training
chunks.text:
Soldiers who have a positive UA or have had an incident that involved a substance abuse issue
must attend and complete ADAPT within calendar 60 days of commander’s notification of the incident.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-14. Alcohol and other drug abuse prevention training
chunks.text:
All Soldiers with an alcohol related incident that was reported on the blotter must complete ADAPT
within 60 days of the incident.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-14. Alcohol and other drug abuse prevention training
chunks.text: Any Soldier referred by a SUD provider who deems ADAPT advisable.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-14. Alcohol and other drug abuse prevention training
chunks.text: The following personnel may attend ADAPT:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-14. Alcohol and other drug abuse prevention training
chunks.text:
Commanders may have a Soldier attend the course for reasons related to poor performance, safety
violations, high risk behaviors, and disciplinary problems.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-14. Alcohol and other drug abuse prevention training
chunks.text: ADAPT training is authorized for civilian personnel and Family members on a space available basis.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 9 > 9-14. Alcohol and other drug abuse prevention training
chunks.text:
Personnel who wish to attend the course for informational purposes only may do so, if approved by
the commander and the ASAP manager.

Installations may request RRP training for their IPTs and installation activities through the
AMC - IMCOM ASAP manager to DPRR.

The DPRR will provide 2 to 3 days of training, based on available funding, on the use of the Com-
mander’s Risk Reduction Toolkit, data analysis, IPT functions, and command briefings.

The PC, in coordination with the ASAP manager and EAPC, will develop ASAP initiatives within the
overall Prevention Plan each fiscal year. The ASAP initiatives in the overall Prevention Plan will be a de-
tailed plan that addresses what and how prevention will occur. The plan will at a minimum address the
following:
Regulatory requirements-such as mandatory training for Soldiers and civilian employees.
Each prevention activity/program/campaign planned for the year describing the following:
The population being targeted.
The activity/program/campaign goals.
The milestones to implementing the activity/program/campaign.
The evaluation method.
Desired outcomes.

Training schedule for UDL certification courses, ADAPT classes, newcomers’ briefings, pre-deploy-
ment/redeployment training, IPT meetings, and so forth.

RRP milestones such as data collection and submission, report printing, IPT meetings and com-
mand briefings.

The ASAP initiatives in the overall Prevention Plan will include universal, selective, and indicated
prevention activities to address the substance abuse prevention needs of the community.

The ASAP initiatives in the overall Prevention Plan, activity evaluations and assessments will be
used as input for the Annual Prevention Report.

Legal and Administrative Procedures, and Media Relations
Section I
General

This chapter addresses legal and administrative actions and procedures involving problematic sub-
stance use and alcohol misuse by Soldiers and civilian employees.
Participation in the SUD treatment need not interfere with normal command administrative actions.

Legal requirements and guidelines for the ASAP must be consistent with the provisions of public
laws, civil and criminal court decisions, DoD issuances, and other ARs. It is essential that the legal equi-
ties involving ASAP are clearly understood at all echelons of command and supervision and that legal
procedures and protections are followed.

All attempts by any means to avoid providing a UA specimen when selected or ordered, to dilute a
urine specimen to reduce the quantitative value of that specimen when confirmed by gas chromatog-
raphy/mass spectrometry (GC/MS), to substitute any substance for one’s own urine, including the urine of
another person, to chemically alter, adulterate, or modify one’s own urine, or to assist another Soldier or
civilian employee in doing any of these actions may violate the Army’s UA policy. Soldiers who violate this
paragraph may be punished under the UCMJ or subject to adverse administrative action. Penalties for
violating this paragraph include the full range of statutory and regulatory sanctions, both criminal and ad-
ministrative. DA civilian employees who violate the regulations related to the Federal Drug-Free Work-
place and DOT Testing Programs may be subject to the full range of disciplinary or adverse administra-
tive actions or both.

Commanders may order a Soldier to provide a specimen for UA if they have probable cause to be-
lieve that illicit drugs are present within the Soldier’s body. Commanders should seek legal counsel before
ordering the urine collection to help them confirm they have probable cause but may order the collection
without counsel if legal counsel is not available. Commanders should subsequently seek legal counsel to
confirm that probable cause existed before using the result in any adverse action.

Supervisors are authorized to test a civilian employee for illegal drug use under the following addi-
tional circumstances:

When an employee is reasonably suspected of having caused or contributed to a work-related acci-
dent or unsafe practice;
As a part of, or as a follow-up to, counseling or rehabilitation for illegal drug use through the EAP.

Supervisors should seek legal counsel before the UA collection to help them confirm there is a rea-
sonable suspicion, but the collection may proceed without legal counsel if none is available. In those
cases, supervisors must subsequently seek legal counsel to confirm that reasonable suspicion existed
before using the drug test result in any adverse action.

A commander may order a UA based upon reasonable suspicion to ensure a Soldier’s fitness for
duty, even if the UA is not a valid inspection and no probable cause exists. However, the results of such a
test may be used only for limited purposes.
